"Almost all people on this board, or at least major part are fat, low in NO and high in LA."

That's a pretty sweeping statement.  You seem to base a lot of assumptions on facade or BMI. By your definition I am fat, but by my definition I'm cognitively improving over time, and if the price I pay to maintain cognitive function is carry more subcutaneous fat than you find aesthetically pleasing, its one I'm willing to pay.  If I was younger it would be one I would also be willing to pay to maintain fertility. The goldilocks zone for fat storage is n=1, and not everyone has the fat mass they have because of processed junk food or the wrong fats, or too much LA, that information has been around for years.

There's a guy spending millions on testing, gadgets and supplements to extend his life, keeping himself really lean. To be fair, he's not recommending anything, but reporting what he is doing, but one red flag is that he has to inject testosterone. There are food influencers who promote various dietary ways of getting healthy, who then come out with videos about why they have changed their minds .... and the latest longevity supplements replace the previous ones that were in vogue.

If someone's good NO status is based on chronically popping supplements, if they don't understand WHY they need it, it doesn't follow that they will reach centenarian age , or maintain good cognitive function. Enough younger people have faced or are facing years of looking after aging parents who have dementia or are being kept alive longer by pharmaceuticals despite poor quality of life. Been there, done that and don't want my kids to have to go through the same thing looking after me, or get the diseases of aging earlier in life themselves. That's more of a motivation than focusing on a few supercentenarians in Sweden when there is no information as to things like their mitochondrial DNA inheritance, womb experience, whether they had happy childhoods, or whether they now have joy, purpose in life, community etc, which also influences biochemistry and longevity.

But famines are much more common that abundance of food.
We only happen to live in periods of abundance.
Abundance of (bad) foods.

### JanSz — 2023-11-04T16:57:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mtor-switch-and-supercentenarians.28243/post-327851>

### habits to borrow from people who've lived to 100​

Only about 20% of how long you live is dictated by your genes, but 80% comes down to lifestyle
journey to a healthy age 100 is joyous
80% overlap where what drives longevity also drives happiness
They do eat meat, but only about five times a month, on average.
They might eat 10 or 11 pounds of meat a year,
compared to 220 pounds for the average American.
Move naturally all day long
.
As U.S. life expectancy declines,
why do some Americans live so much longer and without the ravages of diseases like dementia?
/////////////////////////
yearly 220 pounds of all kinds of meats, animals, birds, fish that is 780 calories per day.
By the same account 11 pounds is 36 calories daily.
\-----
Lets see how this may or may not work.

780-36=744
744 calories when meat replaced by good, low glycemic index vegetable, that could be:

To provide 744 calories from these vegetables,
you would need to consume the following amounts after they are cooked:

-   [Broccoli: 24 cups (2,184 g)](https://www.myfooddata.com/articles/low-calorie-vegetables.php)
-   [Mushrooms: 49.6 cups (3,472 g)](https://www.myfooddata.com/articles/low-calorie-vegetables.php)
-   [Brussels sprouts: 19.6 cups (1,728 g)](https://www.myfooddata.com/articles/low-calorie-vegetables.php)
-   [Kale: 22.5 cups (1,512 g)](https://www.myfooddata.com/articles/low-calorie-vegetables.php)
-   [Carrots: 14.3 cups (1,840 g)](https://www.myfooddata.com/articles/low-calorie-vegetables.php)

Shoot, I like, say mushrooms, but not sure if I could down 3.5 kilo (afraid to convert that to pounds)
\=
Little more manageable could-be legumes:

-   Black beans: 2.8 cups (616 g)
-   Chickpeas: 2.5 cups (500 g)
-   Kidney beans: 2.7 cups (594 g)
-   Lentils: 3.2 cups (704 g)
-   Pinto beans: 2.8 cups (616 g)

There are food influencers who promote various dietary ways of getting healthy, who then come out with videos about why they have changed their minds .... and the latest longevity supplements replace the previous ones that were in vogue.
>
> If someone's good NO status is based on chronically popping supplements, if they don't understand WHY they need it, it doesn't follow that they will reach centenarian age , or maintain good cognitive function. Enough younger people have faced or are facing years of looking after aging parents who have dementia or are being kept alive longer by pharmaceuticals despite poor quality of life. Been there, done that and don't want my kids to have to go through the same thing looking after me, or get the diseases of aging earlier in life themselves. That's more of a motivation than focusing on a few supercentenarians in Sweden when there is no information as to things like their mitochondrial DNA inheritance, womb experience, whether they had happy childhoods, or whether they now have joy, purpose in life, community etc, which also influences biochemistry and longevity.

> **If someone's good NO status is based on chronically popping supplements**, if they don't understand WHY they need it, it doesn't follow that they will reach centenarian age , or maintain good cognitive function.

> but one red flag is that he has to **inject testosterone.**

Testosterone supplementation is my life story. Doing it for decades.
By now I even think that my testicles did me a favor by going out of business.
I was forced to learn so much and was lucky to find DO who was a friendly to humans more than to his wallet.
Eventually it helped me to keep my testosterone levels at the level of 20yo.

.

the gym where Vivian Levy swims every morning is not in Manhattan. [It’s actually located in **Dumbo, Brooklyn, New York**](https://www.essentiallysports.com/us-sports-news-olympics-news-swimming-news-happily-crossing-centurion-one-hundred-four-year-old-woman-explains-how-her-swimming-passion-keeps-her-sane/). [She is a member of the **Life Time Dumbo** gym](https://www.essentiallysports.com/us-sports-news-olympics-news-swimming-news-happily-crossing-centurion-one-hundred-four-year-old-woman-explains-how-her-swimming-passion-keeps-her-sane/). It’s truly inspiring to see her dedication to swimming at her age!

It would be easy to cherry pick examples that fit with what I want or like doing, or where I would prefer to live, but with my n=1 mitochondrial dna inheritance I don't believe I could dance or exercise my way out of my dementia risk anywhere, let alone somewhere like NYC.

### JanSz — 2023-11-07T18:35:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mtor-switch-and-supercentenarians.28243/post-327985>

### [MSN](https://www.msn.com/en-us/health/medical/reversing-your-biological-age-could-help-you-live-longer-and-reduce-dementia-and-stroke-risk-8-habits-to-help-flip-the-switch/ar-AA1juCS7?ocid=msedgntp&pc=HCTS&cvid=b67dbf2f635742ffa04abec5267f394e&ei=28)

www.msn.com

### Reversing your biological age could help you live longer—and reduce dementia and stroke risk. 8 habits to help flip the switch​

Story by Erin Prater • 18h
“If a person's biological age is five years higher than their actual age, the person has a 40% higher risk of developing vascular dementia or suffering a stroke,” Jonathan Mak—a doctoral student in the Department of Medical Epidemiology and Biostatistics at the **Karolinska Institutet in Swede**n—says.© Getty Images
People whose biological age is greater than their chronological age are at a “significantly increased” risk of stroke and dementia—even when smoking, drinking, BMI, and other risk factors are removed from the equation.

That’s according to a [Swedish study published Sunday](https://doi.org/10.1136/jnnp-2023-331917) in the _Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery, and Psychiatry._ Researchers examined the data of more than 325,000 UK residents between the ages of 40 and 70—and neurologically healthy—when the study began.
**They [calculated the biological age](https://fortune.com/well/2023/03/26/mental-illness-health-disorders-could-age-you-slow-clock-biological-depression-anxiety-bipolar/) of each participant via 18 biomarkers, including:**

-   Blood lipids
-   ****Blood sugar****
-   **[**Blood pressure**](https://fortune.com/well/2023/10/10/renters-age-faster-than-homeowners/)**
-   Lung function
-   ****Waist circumference****
-   Red blood cell count
-   Lymphocytes
-   Blood urea nitrogen
-   Creatine
-   Albumin
-   Alkaline phosphatase
-   Glycated hemoglobin
-   Uric acid
-   C-reactive protein

Nine years later, researchers checked to see if participants had developed dementia, stroke, ALS (Lou Gehrig’s disease), or Parkinson’s disease, and if there were any [trends in biological age](https://fortune.com/well/2023/10/10/renters-age-faster-than-homeowners/) among those who had.

Having a higher biological than chronological age seemingly led to an elevated risk of dementia, especially vascular; ischemic stroke, from a blood clot in the brain; and ALS, a neurodegenerative condition, they found.

There was a weaker apparent association between [elevated biological age](https://fortune.com/well/2022/11/10/aging-study-die-sooner-age-faster-live-shorter-study-economy-epigenetic-dna-methylation-clock-age/) and Alzheimer’s disease and other motor neuron diseases, which include progressive spinal muscular atrophy and primary lateral sclerosis

-   The more air a person can expel during a forced breath, the lower the apparent risk of dementia and ischaemic stroke.
-   ****A higher red blood cell count seems to denote an increased risk of dementia.****
-   Women below the age of 60 with an elevated biological age appear to be at the greatest risk of developing dementia.

There did not appear to be a connection between elevated biological age and the development of Parkinson’s disease.

“If a person's biological age is five years higher than their actual age, the person has a 40% higher risk of developing vascular dementia or suffering a stroke,” Jonathan Mak—a doctoral student in the Department of Medical Epidemiology and Biostatistics at the Karolinska Institutet in Sweden, and one of two lead authors on the study—[said in a news release](https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases/1006777) about it.

While the study was an observational one and can’t prove causation, it shows that it may be possible to reduce the number of age-related diseases one develops, or to delay their onset, by improving biomarkers, the authors assert.

## Biological vs. chronological age​

Just what is the difference in biological and chronological age? Simply put, chronological age is how long you’ve been alive—the number of candles on your cake—while biological age is how old your cells are.

Biological age is also referred to as the epigenetic age. The epigenome “consists of chemical compounds that modify, or mark, the genome in a way that tells it what to do, where to do it, and when to do it,” [according to the U.S. National Institutes of Health](https://www.genome.gov/genetics-glossary/Epigenome).

Those changes—influenced by environmental factors like stress, diet, drugs, and pollution—can be passed down from cell to cell as they divide, and from generation to generation. While chronological age can’t be reversed, [biological/epigenetic age can be](https://fortune.com/well/2023/05/17/how-to-lower-biological-age-lifestyle-changes-diet-exercise-relaxation-probiotics/).

Scientists already knew that advanced chronological age is a risk factor for the development of common neurologic disorders like [neuropathy, Alzheimer’s disease, and Parkinson’s disease](https://www.google.com/search?q=age+associated+neurologic+disorders&sca_esv=579903341&rlz=1C1RXQR_enUS1076US1076&sxsrf=AM9HkKn-k59lwMH2xj5GBip7p_tpoZjUHg%3A1699302735605&ei=T01JZdzFJOfg0PEPu6ev6Ag&ved=0ahUKEwicqa__m7CCAxVnMDQIHbvTC40Q4dUDCBA&uact=5&oq=age+associated+neurologic+disorders&gs_lp=Egxnd3Mtd2l6LXNlcnAiI2FnZSBhc3NvY2lhdGVkIG5ldXJvbG9naWMgZGlzb3JkZXJzMgYQABgWGB4yCBAAGIoFGIYDMggQABiKBRiGAzIIEAAYigUYhgNIqzxQqR1Y0TtwA3gBkAEAmAGJAqABlByqAQcxNy4xNS4yuAEDyAEA-_CAgUQIRigAcICBRAhGKsC4gMEGAAgQYgGAZAGCA&sclient=gws-wiz-serp). But because people of the same chronological age, age at different rates, it’s a “rather imprecise measure” when it comes to the prediction of disease development, Sara Hägg—an associate professor in the Department of Medical Epidemiology and Biostatistics at the Karolinska Institutet, and a lead author on the study—[said in the news release](https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases/1006777).

Prior research had shown an apparent correlation between elevated biological age and increased risk of developing some cancers, [depression](https://fortune.com/well/2023/03/26/mental-illness-health-disorders-could-age-you-slow-clock-biological-depression-anxiety-bipolar/), anxiety, and death. But little work had been done on the potential impact of biological age on the development of neurologic disorders, the authors wrote, adding that they next plan to study its impact on other diseases.

## How to slow—and even reverse—biological aging​

The good news: The process of [biological aging can be slowed](https://fortune.com/well/2023/05/19/reverse-aging-brian-armstrong-newlimit-coinbase-epigenetics/)—so much so that one’s biological age can dip below their chronological age.

That’s according to new research released Monday by the American Heart Association. Scientists examined the connection between biological age and the association’s [“Life’s Essential 8” checklist](https://www.heart.org/en/healthy-living/healthy-lifestyle/lifes-essential-8), which includes the goals of:

-   Eating better
-   ****Being more active****
-   Quitting tobacco
-   ****Getting healthy sleep****
-   ****Managing weight****
-   Controlling cholesterol
-   ****Managing blood sugar****
-   ****Managing blood pressure****

After examining the records of more than **6,500 adult participants**, they found that better cardiovascular health—as measured by the above factors—was associated with slower biological aging. Participants with high cardiovascular health had a biological age lower than their chronological age.

For example, the average chronological age of those with high cardiovascular health was 41, but their average biological age was 36, researchers found. Conversely, the average chronological age of those with low cardiovascular health was 53, but their average biological age was 57.

**Participants who scored the highest** on the aforementioned checklist—and thus were considered to have high cardiovascular health—**had a biological age that was, on average, six years younger than their chronological age**, researchers said.

“These findings help us understand the link between chronological age and biological age, and how following healthy lifestyle habits can help us live longer,” Dr. Donald M. Lloyd-Jones, chair of the writing group for the checklist and a past volunteer president of the American Heart Association, said in a news release on the study.

“Everyone wants to live longer—yet more importantly, we want to live healthier longer so we can really enjoy and have a good quality of life for as many years as possible.”

This story was originally featured on [Fortune.com](https://fortune.com/well/2023/11/06/how-to-reverse-biological-epigenetic-age-aging-reduce-stroke-dementia-risk/)

**Journal - conclusion: Thoughts can become things!!! The study shows how the brain can manifest symptoms when the person chronically gives into the assumption that their issue is due to a disability. Is fear a disability?

I Love you!!!**

### Reattribution to Mind-Brain Processes and Recovery From Chronic Back PainA Secondary Analysis of a Randomized Clinical Trial​

[https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2809959](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2809959)

**Beliefs that pain is due to peripheral pathophysiology (eg, a bulging disc, osteoarthritis) are common. Yet, peripheral findings are often incidental in nature and not the predominant cause of symptoms. For patients with primary or nociplastic chronic pain—including the majority of cases of chronic back pain, tension headache, and many other pain conditions—pain is driven predominantly by central upregulation and threat learning processes. For these patients, the inaccurate belief that pain signifies tissue damage may promote fear, avoidance, disuse, and the persistence of pain.**

We recently developed pain reprocessing therapy (PRT), a novel psychological treatment aiming to help patients reframe primary chronic pain as caused by non-dangerous, reversible brain pathways. PRT presents primary pain as what could be described as a “false alarm” of tissue damage that can be reversed. PRT demonstrated promising efficacy in a 2022 clinical trial6 of 151 adults with low-moderate severity chronic back pain: 66% of participants randomized to PRT were pain-free or nearly so at posttreatment, as compared with fewer than 20% of placebo and usual care controls. A better understanding of the psychological mechanisms of PRT is critical.

**In medically unexplained symptom disorders, the misattribution of symptoms to bodily damage or disease is recognized as a central factor driving dysfunction.**

### Jack Kruse — 2023-12-22T16:44:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mtor-switch-and-supercentenarians.28243/post-329409>

Size & Shape is a key feature of any organism since it influences the rate at which resources are consumed and thus affects metabolic rates. In the 1930s, size-dependent relationships were codified as “allometry” and it was shown that most of these could be quantified using the slopes of log-log plots of any 2 variables of interest. During the decades that followed, physiologists explored how animal respiration rates varied as a function of body size across taxa. The expectation was that rates would scale as the 2/3 power of body size as a reflection of the Euclidean relationship between surface area and volume. However, the work of Max Kleiber (1893–1976) and others revealed that animal respiration rates apparently scale more closely as the 3/4 power of body size. This phenomenology, which is called “Kleiber's Law,” has been described for a broad range of organisms, including some algae and plants. Mitochondrial redox power and body size and shape are tightly coupled in the fire of life. It is the rule of oxidative metabolism.

### Jack Kruse — 2023-12-22T16:46:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mtor-switch-and-supercentenarians.28243/post-329410>

In 1789, two French chemists, Antoine Laurent Lavoisier (1743–1794) and Armand Séguin (1767–1835) performed a series of seminal experiments that revolutionized the emerging natural sciences. Assisted by Lavoisier's wife Marie-Anne-Pierette (1758–1836), the research team set up a device to measure the oxygen consumption of a resting human being and discovered that physical activity, as well as the digestion of food, enhances the rate of oxygen uptake. These studies were among the first attempts to quantify what we today call the basal metabolic rate of a living being.

To the best of our knowledge, Lavoisier and Séguin did not relate their oxygen uptake measurements to the body mass of the subject. However, Lavoisier's earlier combustion experiments led to the unequivocal conclusion that both the burning of a candle and the respiratory activity of an animal removed oxygen from the air, a gas he thought of as a chemical element. Hence, Lavoisier concluded in 1780 that metabolism is a natural “internal burning-process” involving the consumption of “live-supporting air” (oxygen, O2) and the release of “fixed air” (carbon dioxide, CO2). Later, he interpreted these findings in the light of his oxygen-theory of combustion. Based on this and many other insights, Lavoisier became one of the founders of modern “anti-phlogistonistic” chemistry, which subsequently gave rise to the physiological sciences.

### Jack Kruse — 2023-12-22T16:49:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mtor-switch-and-supercentenarians.28243/post-329411>

Decades later, the German biologist Eduard Pflüger (1829–1910) discovered that the cells of the body are the sites of biological oxidation, and that the blood transfers O2. Cell respiration was found to be a feature of all living beings (animals and plants), or, as the botanist Julius Sachs (1832–1897) put it, the “combustion of organic substances that provides the energetic basis for the workings of the cells." Based on these insights, Max Rubner (1854–1932) analyzed the relationship between metabolic activity and body mass in mammals.

In 1883, he discovered that mass-specific metabolic rate was ca. 2.5-fold higher in small compared to large dogs, and suggested that a slow metabolism may increase an animal's longevity. tunately th dogs seem to die sooner than smaller dogs. Unfortunately while true in comparing dogs, he noted elephants lived a lot longer than mice and rats and they were a lot larger. He knew there was something missing in this idea. This discovery gave rise to a new area of physiological research that dealt with the relationship between body mass and metabolic activity.

### Jack Kruse — 2023-12-22T16:53:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mtor-switch-and-supercentenarians.28243/post-329412>

First, any valid theory purporting to explain the scaling of body mass with respect to metabolic activity must apply equally to plants as well as to animals, and, second, the literature dealing with this scaling relationship is zoocentric = chloroplast and mitochondria have a bacterial origin.

The size of every organism is intimately correlated with a host of physiological, morphological, anatomical, and life history functional traits in complex but quantifiable ways. Famous biologists, such as Julian Huxley (1887–1975), have sought for a theoretical (mechanistic) explanation for these size-dependent relationships, and 3 major books authored by Francis G. Benedict (1870–1957), Samuel Brody (1889–1956) and Max Kleiber (1893–1976) summarize the state of the art during subsequent decades of ongoing research into the scaling relationship between the basal metabolic rate (_B_) and the body mass (_M_) of animals.

The best known of these scaling relationships is called “Kleiber's Law,” which is described in detail in the book _The Fire of Life_ (1961; 2. ed. 1975). The rule is named in honor of the Swiss/American agricultural scientist Max Kleiber (1893–1976), whose research in the early 1930s indicated that, for the vast majority of mammals, basal metabolic rate _B_ increases as the 3/4 power of total body mass _M_, i.e.,

_B_ = _βM_3/4,

where β is a normalization constant. Thus, according to Kleiber's Law, any animal with a body mass of 150 g will have a basic metabolic rate that is approximately 7.6 times greater than that of an animal with a body mass of 10 g, i.e., metabolic activity of a small organism is, relative to its body size, much higher.

One of the many explanations for Kleiber's Law is that small animals respire more per unit of body mass than larger animals because a larger fraction of their body mass consists of metabolically active tissues that have higher maintenance costs as opposed to reserve tissues that consume comparatively lower resources.

This axiomatic in birds and mammals.

### Jack Kruse — 2023-12-22T17:06:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mtor-switch-and-supercentenarians.28243/post-329415>

Regarding the obesity paradox of supercentenarians: Current clinical guidelines and public health statements generically prescribe body mass index (BMI; 2) categories regardless of the individual’s situation (age, risk for diseases, etc.). However, regarding BMI and mortality rate (MR), two well-established observations are ([1](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3186057/#R1)) there is a U-shaped (i.e., concave) association - people with intermediate BMIs tend to outlive people with higher or lower BMIs; and ([2](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3186057/#R2)) the nadirs of these curves tend to increase monotonically with age.

This offends the smooth brained thinkers.

### Jack Kruse — 2023-12-29T19:28:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mtor-switch-and-supercentenarians.28243/post-329588>

Rapamycin is "thought" to exert its life-extending properties by centralized researchers and clinicians by mimicking the effect of [caloric restriction](https://www.newscientist.com/article/mg20627621-100-eat-less-live-longer/). Not even calorie restriction has been proven to extend life in primates, but don't the silly talking human clinicians this. They believe (scientism) it is one of the most reliable ways to extend lifespan in non-human animals. It targets a signalling molecule called mTOR (an acronym for mechanistic target of rapamycin) which is an important node in our nutrient-sensing pathways. Lack of food switches mTOR off and activates emergency systems that enable us to survive periods of starvation.

These pathways include [autophagy](https://www.newscientist.com/article/2107747-medicine-nobel-prize-goes-to-discovery-of-how-our-cells-recycle/), the process by which cells scavenge dysfunctional organelles and molecules for energy. This reduces the accumulation of the detritus that normally clog up our tissues as we get older, and hence slows or even reverses the ageing process.

Many other mTOR inhibitors are in development for longevity and we should know this decade whether they work as hoped. So far none of the drugs have met the hype. A variety of drugs, including metformin, rapamycin and a host of new senolytics, are being used but we have no idea if they are doing a thing for longevity. Centralized science wants finally showing promise in clearing out zombie cells that cause age-related diseases

So this raises the question in humans.......

Did you know that mTOR is needed for myelin synthesis? You do know myelin is on neurons, huh? So, guys like Peter Attia want you to consider taking an mTOR inhibitor drug for longevity. Do you think you need myelin to think well? To have good cognition? Should you be asking anyone to take an inhibitor of mTOR for optimal cognitive longevity. Like myelin is horrible for the brain, right Dr. Attia? Everyone should want MS disease for longevity huh? If you are human with a Ferrari brain the relies on myelination for optimal function does it make sense to you to use any mTOR inhibitor? You might only get a feww days of life extension but pay for it with a decade of dementia.......

Do you like the trade from the "expert" longevity Dr. Attia?

It is a rhetorical question.

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5836902/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5836902/)

mTOR is a molecular photoswitch. Nothing in biochemistry operates without the light that controls it. Molecular photoswitches function in cells to both convert and store energy for optical signaling. They are used in cells to make solar energy harvesting more efficient.

Modern technology teaches us that solar energy can be either used directly to generate electricity or indirectly via the energy stored in heat reservoirs.

Ancient quantum photosynthesis biology tells us that Mother Nature favors a 3rd route that modern humans are ignorant of. The third route involves storing the sun's energy in light-sensitive materials and releasing it as needed in a cell. Vitamin A does this, and so does cholesterol in cells.

Modern tech people are just finding out about this science. You should read up on molecules such as bicyclic dienes to understand what Nature knows and what food gurus do not.

CITE

Electricity In the UK is 240V 50Hz AC and every wire running through the walls floor and ceiling puts out an electric field 6 - 8 feet in all directions. To create an EMF (electric & magnetic fields) mitigated sleep sanctuary you want to identify the circuits that affect your bed area and turn them off at the breaker panel, not just unplug stuff. We prefer the body voltage method to test for electric fields because it shows whats going on in your body. You would need a UK/USA travel adaptor (Curries sells them). We have had some wonderful, kind, caring doctors over the years who took care of our family.
One, in particular tho- a surgeon - was a total scumbag........ and my wonderful husband was nothing more than a meal ticket or his next trip to Europe and a bigger and more expensive car.
We saw many doctors on a regular basis in our restaurant and so many of them gave us their private phone numbers when my dear husband was so very sick and we were told to call if we needed help - any time day or night.

So after all these years of reading Jacks blogs .....I wasn't really understanding that a lot of Doctors are not interested in "doing no harm" .
Their only interest is in a big house and an expensive car or two or three and all the luxuries life has to offer

How could I have been so naive for so many years......

There is no shame in having to reread material, especially high level material like Kruse is teaching. I keep going back to read the hormone 101 blog (mainly bc it is recommended to do so frequently throughout other blogs), and each time I go back, I am reminded of important things I have forgotten.

Never had UTI after that either. I am in love with German Doctors because I have a feeling many of them are doctors of the heart. That is at least my own personal experience. Absolute thrilled with every doctor experience I had there the 18 years I lived there!

Do nor beat yourself up.
**There are many situations that you will be unable to stay away from Cipro.**
So, there is always die now or die latter choice.
For number of years, I had to take Cipro before many of mine, prostate biopsies.
That was with my old urologist.
He was a gentle guy. Then retired.
With biopsy there was 12 shots to take samples from desirable places.
So, after each shot, he spends some time watching how I am doing.
This (current urologist), I think he had machine gun, brum brum, go home.
At home I found that I am losing blood.
Called him,
he does not want to listen much, just go to hospital.
I spend 3 days in hospital, they did nothing to me, just watching.
On a way out they told me that I was really close to getting transfusion of new blood.

### evan12321 — 2023-10-15T17:45:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-covid-patient-locations-and-doctor-help.28249/post-327291>

Hi Mitochondriacs,

I'm a Long Covid patient, currently in the northeast, looking at options for a change in environment. Dr. Kruse had some suggestions and also suggested reaching out in the forum.

If you live in or are familiar with places in the southern US, especially the gulf states, with low population density but proximity to decent medical care, any advice/tips would be appreciated. If you know a GP who is staying up to date on Long Covid, even better (I have been checking the FLCCC).

I may have to start with a move to family in Savannah, GA and improve things from there, although they're closer to center city than would be ideal.

### Sue-UK — 2023-10-16T13:59:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-covid-patient-locations-and-doctor-help.28249/post-327311>

I've used chlorella with a high spermidine content, because natural spermidine levels in cells decrease with age. I've read that naked mole rats don't get the same spermidine age related decline as other rodents of the same size, and they live 10 times as long.

There seems to be a link between covid disrupting the spermidine pathways in cells. I'm a visual learner and found this quite a good video explaining the mechanisms behind it and the mechanisms by which spermidine, resveratrol and IF can help.

### JanSz — 2023-10-16T16:25:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-covid-patient-locations-and-doctor-help.28249/post-327317>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=327311)

>
> There seems to be a link between covid disrupting the spermidine pathways in cells. I'm a visual learner and found this quite a good video explaining the mechanisms behind it and the mechanisms by which spermidine, resveratrol and IF can help.
>

Nice video, thank you.
That video ends with two advices:
using drugs, spermidine and or resveratrol
and
natural ways: **intermittent fasting, IF.**
\-------------------------------------
**I would like to think and research about IF.**

I am in the process of studying mTOR.

[mTOR is a survival mechanism that signals to the body and cells that they should](https://youthandearth.com/blogs/blog/mtor-longevity-pathway)
[**grow during times of plenty**](https://youthandearth.com/blogs/blog/mtor-longevity-pathway)
[and](https://youthandearth.com/blogs/blog/mtor-longevity-pathway)
[**not grow when food is scarce**](https://youthandearth.com/blogs/blog/mtor-longevity-pathway)
\------
During food scarce time energy need to keep body alive is made using
body fat, and bad stuff which may be the stuff discussed in above video and additionally all kind of starting cancer cells, diabetes and other undesirables,
and when above is gone
using fat in the bone marrow to continue on living.
\---------
Above this type of discussion newer mentions
the amount of body fat body has in good times.
and the amounts of body fat during cleanup
also
that at certain point, when fasting lasts too long, body dies.
\---------
The pictures of fat people that we often see are misleading.
They show people morbidly obese; they are not healthy people, they are sick.

I am looking for a maximum body fat percentage of people **who are healthy but about ready to go for a fast.**

In my estimation that is about 10-15%
Anything above that should be considered morbidly obese and not desirable.

I have seen discussions stating that healthy Body Mass Index is about 18-24.
That does not say much about body fat, because some people have a more other less muscles.
But
in my personal case, until few days ago I was thinking of myself as a lean person, but no more.
I am 5' 8" 150#
my body fat varies from 15% to 13%
my BMI=22.8
if I would lose 10# my BMI would be 21.29
another 10#, so having 130# BMI=19.77
still far from 18
\===============
This part needs to be clarified better.
Because
250# or 200# person (many if not most here are those) may THINK THAT HE/SHE is fasting
while she really should lose another almost 100#
**before their mTOR is turned down, when using Intermittent Fasting.**

////////////////////////

Years ago, I knew a healthy, muscled volunteer fireman who was refused tandem skydiving because he was over the BMI. Someone skinny, less fit and perhaps with weaker bones and a higher risk of injury in the event of a bad landing technique, was allowed to do the jump. That exposed the flaws, so I'm not too interested in BMI. My body type holds more than the average subcutaneous fat when healthy, and my risk is greater from excess visceral fat. Within reason, I see my SQ fat as protective, and I can still get the benefits of IF.

_Jack: "The skin is a cholesterol laden insulator of fat with lipid raft antenna's designed to connect us to the sun.........."

"That external light determines the reality the mammal faces. When the solar cycles change so do the lipid rafts. This photoelectric change alters biochemistry in **mTOR**, PPP, glycolysis, the TCA cycle, and POMC cleavage. When the lipid content changes they induce changes in the semiconductive proteins embedded in them. This changes the physiologic ability. This is why the clock mechanism in mammals is linked to **light and temperature.** Both signals change to the surfaces of mammals."_

([https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/podcasts-dropbox-collection.18701/page-2#post-321555](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/podcasts-dropbox-collection.18701/page-2#post-321555))

So if I follow the sun, and experience the cold in winter (living in the cold lane and CT) I think I'll do better than starving myself to get closer to a BMI that suits any conventional mTOR paradigm. I know I don't fit with many people's ideas of what I should weigh, or how much weight I should lose, but if that's based on facade or BMI, they aren't going to be packing my parachute any time soon.

### Sue-UK — 2023-10-17T14:50:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-covid-patient-locations-and-doctor-help.28249/post-327358>

[@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/)

_"I am looking for a maximum body fat percentage of people **who are healthy but about ready to go for a fast.**_

_In my estimation that is about 10-15%
Anything above that should be considered morbidly obese and not desirable."

"my body fat varies from 15% to 13%"

"I hate being hungry probably more than you do."_

I think there's a difference between inflammation or addiction driven hunger, and a hunger message that's a signal to replace lost mass.

In the case of covid, if the spike proteins disrupt the autophagy machinery, having a very low body fat percentage is not necessarily an advantage, it could just be a different risk, even in the young. In terms of recovery from covid and long covid, having extremely low levels of SQ fat could result in losing the _"...... lipid raft antenna's designed to connect us to the sun.........."_ with the subsequent photoelectric mTor connection. This could have knock on effects for anyone trying to use the sun to recover from Covid or long covid. or just using the sun to remain healthy, even if their environment in terms of nnEMF is good. If leptin resistance can occur in anorexia as well as obesity, I suspect there will be an n=1 point where fasting (other than overnight) for those with low body fat will be contra indicated. As fat is an endocrine organ, the healthy may become unhealthy in terms of natural vitamin D production, sex hormones and fertility etc etc if they try and reach an unrealistic body fat percentage. When losing too much mass, there's also potential for having insufficient deuterium which is used by immune cells. I think there is much more to it than estimating that 10 to 15% body fat is OK but anything over 15 per cent is morbidly obese. That doesn't even reach half truth status .....

but
I remember the goal under which it was stated.
That goal is to live until 80yo.

For me:
**supercentenarians** this is nice goal worth trying
For me the **most eye-catching** statement about mTOR was that:

During food scarce time, energy need to keep body alive is made using
body fat, **and bad stuff** which may be the stuff discussed in above video and additionally **all kind of starting cancer cells, diabetes and other undesirables,**
My mom died at 95

For a 3 gram serving of the 50/50 mix, only 1.5 grams is chlorella, so the spermidine dose would be minimal, even if the pyrenoidosa is the right strain. Where I buy it from the regular chlorella is pyrenoidosa, but the chlorella promoting the spermidine content I don't know which strain it is, but as they are the same price I haven't gone into it further, I just buy the one where the spermidine is measured.

Its just a musing on my part  but if spermidine synthase is disrupted by the virus or the spike proteins (inc from jabs), then putrescine is less likely to be converted to spermidine, so during infection or dealing with long covid, temporarily cutting down on putrescine from dietary intake and by doing IF and adding a source of spermidine, if it has the potential of salvaging autophagy to help clear the spike proteins, for me its worth a go, particularly because of the other nutrients. But how I do it and how much to take is n=1, particularly because of my age. I calculated roughly my intake from the summer seasonal epi paleo, and I can adjust the dose as my diet changes over the winter. But if I became infected or was suffering from long covid, I'd be changing things up, particularly as the quantum yield is dropping like a stone.

I totally understand. Next is just a personal observation and not directed at anyone. In almost 35 years of treating patients, I have seen and learned a lot. One lesson I have learned is to care for others as much as you feel is necessary, but do it without neglecting yourself or to the detriment of yourself. I have seen many patients come in due to helping/caring, at their expense. By the time they come in a lot of damage was done and a lot of work will be needed to reverse it. When the oxygen mask drops place it over your nose and mouth first, before assisting others.

### Jack Kruse — 2023-10-28T11:25:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-october-2023-q-a-was-long-but-it-had-nuggets-on-light-stability.28254/post-327597>

How can you master high latitude living?

Learn about the physics of organisms!

What do most members not understand about high and low latitude living?

Going to bed just 90 minutes past your regular bedtime means cells that line your blood vessels are flooded by damaging oxidants. And unlike well-rested cells, sleep-restricted cells fail to activate antioxidant responses to clear the destructive molecules. "Sleep restriction markedly increased endothelial oxidative stress without upregulating antioxidant response."

So just going to El Salvador over Toronto improves your health. It is not Uncle Jack hyperbole. It is YOUR lack of understanding of the physics or organisms and your addiction to current wants needs and desires that blocks your progress.

**CHANGE THAT**

You heard what what of my members said LIVE on the October Q & A. Soon you will all see what I said to Owen Sheasby and the two Vikings on the recent podcast.

This effect is all related to Einstein's version of time versus Newton's version of absolute time.

The “eye clock” creates time from the interaction between light and the pi electrons in DHA. That current of flow is directed by a large push in voltage by purple and blue light through the central retino-hypothalamic tracts that enter our SCN. That interaction is the basis of how circadian biology is coordinated.

_**Consider this fact:**_ Half the bestselling drugs in the US target the product of circadian genes, yet big pharma continues to ignore chronobiology at our peril. Why? They are interested in customer retention and not customer healing.

_**Why height/altitude is bad in a blue-lit world devoid of purple and red solar light:**_ This relationship cements why any altitude, off Earth’s surface, seems to deadly for living things. Living in a high rise building, flying constantly, or space travel will be a prescription for term life insurance. It is not an Rx for pills, surgery, or a dietary fix. The drugs that Big Pharma uses affect the timing mechanisms are static, and don’t vary with the waves in our environment, so people have to continue to rely on drugs instead of using light to innovate solutions to environmental light problems.

[https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1713958035045920819](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1713958035045920819)

When this mis-timing occurs in our retina’s mitochondria ECT, it results in enlargement of our respiratory proteins of the central retina pathways where melanopsin is the gatekeeper photoreceptor. This ruins the neural signaling in the leptin melanocortin pathways. Since swelling of one Angstrom slows ECT speeds by a factor of ten, SCN timing ability slows while the peripheral clocks speed up on a relative basis. As ECT slows electron flow it causes inaccurate timing. Bad timing results in things like neuro-degeneration, [T2D](http://www.northwestern.edu/newscenter/stories/2015/11/circadian-clock-controls-insulin-and-blood-sugar-in-pancreas.html), and autoimmunity in our tissues where the timing mechanism is sped up. This is why chronic blue light exposure at night causes low melatonin levels in our eye first at the SCN and the habenular nucleus where our frontal lobe relay station of melanin exists. You'll hear about that in the Tetragrammaton #3 podcast between Rick, Bill, and myself. Light frequencies must be accurately coupled to the retina and brain to tell proper time. It is also why red light condenses respiratory proteins to make more ATP, because shrinking ECT size, increases the current of electron flow causing time dilation. Time shrinks when this occurs, and healing and regeneration can occur. The relationship of distance in these cytochrome proteins to the current of electron flow becomes asymmetric under the rule of [General and Special relativity](http://www.beastified.com/bp1/2015/9/19/bp3-dr-jack-kruse), and this asymmetry underpins how time emerges and is perceived in that particular tissue.

**CITES**

### [HIGH LATITUDE LIVING 101](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/high-latitude-living-101-jack-kruse-mz3te/)

WHY DOES my recent @owensheasby PODCAST MATTER DEEPLY TO THOSE WHO REMAIN IN THE DARK ON QUANTUM BIOLOGY? If you consider the mass-energy equivalence relationship, E= mc^2 you might be led to this conclusion. However, what this equation really tells us is that matter is not conserved in Nature.

# Thread: UK Bottled Water
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mackerelx10 — 2023-10-17T22:36:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/post-327370>

Hey,

I live in the South West of England. I've been avoiding drinking tap water for about a year now, mostly drinking Highland Spring instead. Main reasons being it's from a high latitude, hasn't travelled too far and is available in enclosed 10L boxes so less exposure to light in transit etc. Anyway, I've just read on an older thread that someone had it tested for heavy metals and it was high in barium, so thinking time to switch. Also read that Evian has the lowest level of deuterium, at least out of the ones I saw that are easily available in the UK.

Another I've drank a fair bit is Brecon Carreg which is even more local to me but sourced at a lower latitude. I think the boxed Highland Spring was what swayed my decision til now but I'm sure there's more important things to consider. I'm fairly new to this stuff but picking things up as I go, just trying to figure out the best options for me right now (living in a city on a tight budget). Any opinions or useful tips would be greatly appreciated!

Blessings

### Sue-UK — 2023-10-19T10:09:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/post-327416>

Hi

I was living in the South West til a couple of years ago, now in the South East.

Fiddling around with a few ppm deuterium between different UK bottled waters isn't one of my criteria, particularly on a tight budget. Avoiding deuterium loaded crap food or supplements is higher on my list as is improving the endogenous deuterium depleting pathways. I don't use one brand of water, at the moment I'm using 3 - I call it going to a different watering hole.  One is a general UK bottled spring water - "not tap water" brand, even that varies depending on which supermarket I use, and what's on offer, the other 2 are for therapeutic purposes, based on my age and n=1.

### [25 HydroHealth (24 bottles x 500ml Box) $190 incl.S&H-no PO box- Choos](https://25-ddw-hydrohealth.myshopify.com/products/25-hydrohealth-24pcs-x-500ml-box)

Best Price: $190 for one case, 24 bottles, 16.90 fl. oz. (500 ML) each, $7.90 per bottle, including S&H. Subscribe and save an additional 10%($19). GET ONE BOX MONTHLY FOR ONLY $171 ($7.12 per bottle)- SHIPPING INCLUDED Please also review our subscription policy. Visit "About Subscriptions" No...

25-ddw-hydrohealth.myshopify.com

I buy my DDW water from the place above.
They sell variety 25, 12, and 50ppm D/H
but the 25ppm works the best for me.
I drink on average 125ppm water.
That is a 4 half liter bottles of RO water and one half liter 25ppm
\=(1\*25+4\*150)/5=125
My tests and other info are on this thread:

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-make-ddw-do-you-buy-this-50ppm.20927/page-8#post-239806](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-make-ddw-do-you-buy-this-50ppm.20927/page-8#post-239806)

I know that that water works because **for three years I have been drinking exclusively** that 25ppm water and numerous times tested how it works for me by testing.
Before I started drinking all that I made a test to see my starting situation.
I tested condensate of my breath and saliva.
They were much higher on the beginning at progresively lower in deuterium latter on.
I have posted my results on this board.
Working with the outfit that initially had dr Boros helping them, I have found that:

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/post-327480>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=327416)
>
> Hi
>
> I was living in the South West til a couple of years ago, now in the South East.
>
> Fiddling around with a few ppm deuterium between different UK bottled waters isn't one of my criteria, particularly on a tight budget. Avoiding deuterium loaded crap food or supplements is higher on my list as is improving the endogenous deuterium depleting pathways. I don't use one brand of water, at the moment I'm using 3 - I call it going to a different watering hole.  One is a general UK bottled spring water - "not tap water" brand, even that varies depending on which supermarket I use, and what's on offer, the other 2 are for therapeutic purposes, based on my age and n=1.

Sue what are the three you buy and also are they low in minerals for putting in the freezer to drink from regarding the deuterated water?

### Sue-UK — 2023-10-23T09:32:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/post-327489>

> [Josie Thomson said:](/goto/post?id=327480)
>
> Sue what are the three you buy and also are they low in minerals for putting in the freezer to drink from regarding the deuterated water?

I have done that method before, but don't have the freezer space now, and I don't think the amount depleted makes it worth it. I use a silicon rich mineral water to deplete aluminum, which is implicated in DH and my maternal mitochondrial DNA risks, heart disease and dementia, and Jack has talked about aluminum's reflecting properties. So getting aluminum out has the potential to improve endogenous light signalling etc. The other one I use is an oxygenated water mentioned by Que Collins (who was one of the co-founders of the Centre of Deuterium Depletion) who said in an interview (interview and transcript at [https://melanieavalon.com/water/](https://melanieavalon.com/water/))

_"Then, we use water as a way to get you there. But here's what I think, is what I'm really happy about, is using this new Kure oxygenated water, it's very, very inexpensive. It's only about $2 a bottle, so it's the same price as a bottle of Fiji water, Perrier water, whatever, but you're able to use that.

If you think about the deuterium-depleted water, it is what I like to call passive depletion. In other words, you're diluting-- you're trying to get everything lower and in time, because it's lower, it's going to make you better. With Kure oxygenated water, it’s just the opposite. It's active. It's like having a fighter plane. What it's going to do, you're going to drink it, these ultra-fine bubbles are going to float through, and we have pictures and everything of how it works. It is pretty amazing. It's going to flow to your organs, get into your mitochondria, and then bind a hydrogen deuterium, and remove it from the system. **What's nice is oxygen has an affinity about six times or seven times the amount of affinity for deuterium than it does for hydrogen. It's going to bind to deuterium first,** that's one of the reasons that we breathe. Believe it or not, remind me to talk about that. But oxygen binds deuterium to make heavy water, and it's heavy, because remember deuterium is twice as heavy, so it's physically heavy, and our body has all these systems built in to specifically get rid of this heavy water. "_

I think there's a possibility that they also work synergistically in removing aluminum and deuterium.

When I get a drink of water, I mix 400ml of cold silicon rich water (currently Volvic as its easily available in the supermarkets) with 100ml of cold oxygenated water and drink it fairly quickly, and use the "not tap water" for anything that is going to be heated such as hot drinks or cooking. The oxygenated water is spring water from Cornwall infused with oxygen, and that's the only one I get delivered. Its currently marketed for sports recovery, where someone might drink a whole bottle after a workout, but I'm not sure about that for deuterium depletion for my n=1. The DD centre are conducting a trial on its use in covid, and says
_"This could possibly be happening since Kure delivers 100 times more oxygen than regular water in tiny, stable, ultrafine bubbles."_ which is far more oxygen than the other oxygenated water research I've read**,** which is why I cut it to the ratio I use, because I'm not dealing with cancer etc or anything covid related.

([https://www.ddcenters.com/kure-covid-study2/](https://www.ddcenters.com/kure-covid-study2/))

At £24.99 for 24 x 500ml bottles and £1 for 1 1/2 liters of volvic its more affordable than DDW.

Thank you!  I love the fact that you have wonder woman intelligence! Hi Sue, I also live in the U.K and am looking for affordable deuterium depleted water options - interseted to know if you felt any significant improvements form drinking Kure? I loathe the idea of adding more microplastics to my body and it seems so many of these waters are in plastic bottles. Have you by any chance stumbles upon anyone that does a glass bottled version of good water. i currently drink alot of San Pelligrino as it's at the lower end of deuterium and it seems to hydrate me better than anything else. Thanks A

### Chlo_e — 2024-07-23T11:21:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/post-337510>

Another option is to collect spring water if you happen to live near one of the springs- I've attached the link to find a spring (you just have to navigate to the UK using the cursor on the map rather than clicking on the dropdown menu).
[https://findaspring.org/map/](https://findaspring.org/map/)

We now take dark glass bottles (dark blue, specifically) and collect spring water once a week or once a fortnight, no plastic involved.

Thanks A

I've continued using the same waters since my post in October 23. I've accepted that although what I buy is in plastic bottles, the advantages of aluminium removal and moderate deuterium depletion and avoiding tap water outweighs the negatives for my n=1.

Although I'm still using Kure, I am less picky about measuring it out with 400ml of water and 100ml Kure, mainly just use it intuitively.

The way I look at it is that if all the deuterium in my body suddenly vanished, I'd probably be a puddle on the floor - so my messing about with excessive deuterium depletion isn't without risk. Deuterium in the wrong place such as the mitochondria is another issue, which is one reason I use Kure. The other reason I use Kure is a pic Jack put in my journal when I mentioned the oxygenated water hack ..

[https://static.webshopapp.com/shops...08/pakdaman-studies-oxygen-enriched-water.pdf](https://static.webshopapp.com/shops/035143/files/056059208/pakdaman-studies-oxygen-enriched-water.pdf)
Oxygen enriched water and Oral Oxygen Therapy (OOT)

Fig.2: A reduction of O2 content in the blood is observed with increasing age.

(This could also affect deuterium depletion).

Herrera Human Photosynthesis book says that we begin to lose our capacity to split and reform the water molecule (i.e. a role of melanin) at around 26, losing function at an approx rate of 10 per cent with each decade after mid twenties, and it goes into free fall when we reach our 50s.

Doug Wallace heteroplasmy worsening 10 per cent per decade .....

So there's the deuterium angle, but my decision to continue using it is also based on oxygen and my being in my mid sixties .... Its difficult to quantify the effects of one water when its in a combination with everything else I do, but if it mitigates some of the deuterium/less blood O2 risks because of my age its worth it for me.

Sue, what are your thoughts on H2 O2 diluted in water.. I've been trying it out in our small essential oil diffuser, it doesn't feel like it's doing much.... Thinking it helps with a similar effect to what your involved in here... but I don't know about the overall benefits this way tbh.

### Sue-UK — 2024-07-25T17:46:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/post-337563>

> [Josie Thomson said:](/goto/post?id=337558)
>
> Sue, what are your thoughts on H2 O2 diluted in water.. I've been trying it out in our small essential oil diffuser, it doesn't feel like it's doing much.... Thinking it helps with a similar effect to what your involved in here... but I don't know about the overall benefits this way tbh.

I don't think the effects of hydrogen peroxide is similar to what I'm hacking.  Although most of what I've seen is warning against the risks from people using it in nebulizers, rather than diffusing it into the air from an essential oil diffuser, hydrogen peroxide via the lungs is not something I'd play with on my own without really understanding what it was doing. For example some bacteria such as _Streptococcus pneumoniae_ produce hydrogen peroxide.

_"Here, we demonstrated that S. pn-secreted H2O2 caused mitochondrial damage and severe histopathological damage in mouse lung tissue. Additionally, S. pn-secreted H2O2 caused not only oxidative damage to mitochondrial deoxyribonucleic acid (mtDNA),. but also a reduction in the mtDNA content in alveolar epithelia cells. "_

### [Frontiers | Mitochondrial DNA Leakage Caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae Hydrogen Peroxide Promotes Type I IFN Expression in Lung Cells](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/microbiology/articles/10.3389/fmicb.2019.00630/full)

Streptococcus pneumoniae (S. pn), the bacterial pathogen responsible for invasive pneumococcal diseases, is capable of producing substantial amounts of hydro...

 www.frontiersin.org

Although the paper is about mice lung tissue, and what the experiment was may make a difference blah blah, it makes me cautious, although a small essential oil diffuser in a room is possibly not doing much depending on the overall air volume etc.

Hey Sue, when it comes to the deuterium content of supplements, is there any rule of thumb of which ones are worse? I am assuming ones with fillers are more of the problem? Also, do you know if taking collagen and gelatin are high deuterium as know that there is often where the body is naturally higher in deuterium. Many thanks

### Sue-UK — 2024-07-26T17:50:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uk-bottled-water.28259/post-337599>

> [Annelieuk said:](/goto/post?id=337591)
>
> Hey Sue, when it comes to the deuterium content of supplements, is there any rule of thumb of which ones are worse? I am assuming ones with fillers are more of the problem? Also, do you know if taking collagen and gelatin are high deuterium as know that there is often where the body is naturally higher in deuterium. Many thanks

It gets too complicated for me .... There's fillers, the manufacturing process, what an animal ate for an animal derived product etc, and added to Jack's SUNplement not supplement, its something I try to avoid. But if I chose to take a supplement, its the context again ....from a 30,000 ft view would some deuterium in a supplement be such an issue in an otherwise low deuterium intake? That might even vary seasonally. My bigger issue is that if a capsule or tablet is swallowed with water, the gut and the body gets what it gets, whether it can deal with it or not, without the signalling from the mouth I'd get from a whole real food. I crave something until I don't. At the moment I use spirulina and chlorella, which are grown as crops, and IP6 gold which I get in powder form to mix in water and a dash of ACV rather than capsules. I can taste these things, which I think can give advance notice of what is going down the chute, and stop if its not right.

# Thread: European Member Cruise Oct -Nov

### Dr Rohen Kapur — 2023-10-18T21:50:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-member-cruise-oct-nov.28262/post-327398>

If anyone else is going on the cruise could they post a reply here.

22nd October to November 4th

We can watch the sunrise and sunsets together. ( Despite the weather forecasts of drizzle)

### Jack Kruse — 2023-10-18T21:51:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-member-cruise-oct-nov.28262/post-327399>

Very few people signed up unfortunately.

### Jack Kruse — 2023-10-18T21:55:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-member-cruise-oct-nov.28262/post-327402>

Many people are coming to see me in London I believe in 10/20-23 before we board. Several things have changed but if you contact Christi she can hook you up directly with Sarah Hogdson who is running the London ground show for me.

We can meet up before the cruise if you'd like. I have no idea what they have planned for me to do.

### Dr Rohen Kapur — 2023-10-18T23:07:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-member-cruise-oct-nov.28262/post-327406>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=327399)
>
> Very few people signed up unfortunately.

Thats disappointing. I needed a holiday ( plus I guess having a cruise in the middle of half term school holiday was perchance risky. ) But it got me out of another disaster that I'd booked for October November . I jumped (to the) ship Literally. London is probably going to be rather tricky in terms of logistics ( as its a good three hour journey by car or 2 hours by train to centre ) but I think I'll just enjoy the cruise for what it is... A cruise. I'll certainly see you if you rejoin the ship in Ponta Delgada ( at the Azores)

Have messaged Sarah on Facebook and Christy. Will see if I can logistically do anything though I think thats rather improbable. London and Southampton are at least an hour plus distance between them traffic dependent. Could try to come into London via Southampton on the Saturday night though I may not have the redox available for such a trip.

Nikki is on the cruise as she just indicated to me. it was why I started this thread in the first place. ( Shucks ) Life, However I couldnt pass up arriving in Manhattan by ship. and the route doesnt have any icebergs I believe.....

Edit: it is probably impossible to get to London from Southampton by the time I arrive there. ( not enough time) Better that I rest up before the cruise eat and sleep. By the time I got to London I'd have to catch a train back. Not to mention the awful weather that they have forecast the rest of this week. And weekend trains ( not risking that.)

I havent been on a cruise since I did one in 1999 from Singapore. for a course during the cruise it was on the Star Asia. I enjoyed it and never got off the boat I didnt actually get off on the excursion to Thailand but I got a stamp in the passport. But it was peaceful. and I remember sleeping well on the boat, again I slept well on an over night ferry in 2015 Now of course I know why. No radio waves in the bowel of a ship. Once the light is off the light and the electromagnetic spectrum is completely gone. ( having said that I booked a cabin with a window) but its going to be dark from 6-7 pm onwards till about 6am

Edit The Em spectrum is significantly reduced. there are no 3g 2g 4 g and 5 g transmitters in the middle of the atlantic ( well I hope not)

### Marko Pollo — 2023-10-20T01:05:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-member-cruise-oct-nov.28262/post-327438>

I hope someone brings some meters to test and then posts results. I spent a lot of time at sea on fishing boats from 28-60 ft all the way up to aircraft carriers, sometimes sleeping within feet of pretty big gen sets. on one boat, the radar dome was probably 2 ft above my head while piloting, separated by an 1/8 of aluminum. We used to joke half heartedly about it making some body parts fall off but they're all still there.

### Anthony Smith — 2023-10-20T02:51:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-member-cruise-oct-nov.28262/post-327441>

Taking some meters would be interesting BUT sometimes “ignorance is bliss”. If you can’t do anything about it maybe just enjoy the vacation and inform everyone of the results later. If you can avoid it for example by blocking a WiFi router above or next to your head while you sleep, then share the info. I’ve heard some of these ships have the “latest and greatest” 5G, it would be worth knowing if they do where they are and avoid those areas. Not even sure how a high mm 5G frequency would work throughout a metal boat unless it is only offered in certain areas . . . they would be the areas to avoid.

### Marko Pollo — 2023-10-20T04:17:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/european-member-cruise-oct-nov.28262/post-327443>

I have taken my meters into hydro electric plants and up below giant antennae arrays and been surprised by the low levels of emf. On the other hand, I've seen simple but faulty light fixtures in my house nonchalantly emitting 500 times what I experienced in a hydro plant. When we travel, we always use meters to find sources of emf and turn them off if possible, but never damaging anything. Unplug hidden router and replug it before leaving. We cover any small lights with black tape. We never shit our pants if there is a tower too close or the room is less than optimal, just move on tomorrow. If one is emf sensitive, I'd be cautious on a ship because there could be giant powerlines running through your stateroom and you wouldn't know without meters or sensitivity. Or, it could be totally nothing to worry about. I feel like travelling with meters also shows how I'm doing in my own environment at home. I'd rather know the truth.

Found this the other day...

_**SpaceX's network of low-orbit satellites emits "unintended electromagnetic radiation"**_

### [What do we have to lose if Starlink satellites and others like them take over the night sky? Studying dark matter, observing distant galaxies, and finding signs of extraterrestrial intelligence.](https://www.businessinsider.com/elon-musk-starlink-satellites-leaking-unintended-radiation-spacex-study-2023-7)

47 of 68 Starlink satellites observed produced unintentional radiation due to onboard electronics. Scientists suspect other satellites have the same issue.

 www.businessinsider.com

### [Elon Musk’s Starlink satellites are leaking radiation, study warns](https://www.independent.co.uk/space/starlink-radiation-astronomy-spacex-elon-musk-b2372560.html)

SpaceX’s space internet network already has more than 4,000 satellites

### markthesunbather — 2023-10-19T17:06:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-st-augustine.28263/post-327418>

Long time lurker here. Ten years ago -around the time I turned 25- I had some pretty scary health issues and somehow, someway, I discovered Jack and his work. Even ordered his book on Amazon back then, though I have no idea who turned me onto it (not sure anyone even did; it's like the universe offered it up to me or something). Been listening to every podcast he's done that I can find; gotten to where I'm also searching his name on YouTube regularly for new offerings. Stuff gets more interesting with each passing year. Also trying to go back and listen to the Q&As/powows from yesteryear whenever opportunity presents. I find the information endlessly fascinating, even though I'm nowhere near as sharp as I was half my life ago and so much of it seems beyond me. Much love to you all.

# Thread: Let's talk organic.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-organic.28267/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JanSz — 2023-10-21T19:52:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-organic.28267/post-327460>

Everybody knows that canola oil is undesirable, at least because of its very high LA, Linoleic Acid content.
There are other problems there to.
But,
**Organic food may be the best we can muster**.
Maybe, maybe not.
Maybe organic is not as good as they want you to think about.
\---------------------------------------------------
Canola oil is made by crushing the seeds of the canola plant.
After oil is extracted from canola seeds, the remaining by-product is a protein-rich meal.
Extracting oil from canola seeds results in two products: canola oil and a high-protein co-product known as canola meal.
So, nothing goes to waste! It’s an efficient use of resources.
\---
Trust me, there are no canola oil in canola meal.
\---
Animal Feed: Canola meal is one of the **most widely used** protein sources in animal feed for livestock, poultry, and fish.
\---
[Yes, **canola meal legally can be fed to organic swine and poultry**](https://eorganic.org/node/8097) **and fish.**
\---
Caveats (do you really care about those details)
Inclusion Levels:
Studies suggest that canola meal might need to be restricted during the starter period to 20% for broilers and turkeys,
and 10% for more exotic ducks, geese and quail.
Concentrations of 30% to 40% of the diet are readily tolerated at later stages of growth.
Note:
**The above is about organic food**.
...
Yes, canola meal can be fed to **organic cows.**
Canola meal is **widely** used in diets for **dairy and beef cattle.**
It is considered to be a premium ingredient.
...

### JanSz — 2023-10-21T20:17:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-organic.28267/post-327461>

And there is another surprise.
Yes, soybean meal can be used to feed **organic** livestock, pigs, poultry, and fish.

...

### JanSz — 2023-10-21T22:23:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-organic.28267/post-327462>

## **How Vegetable Oils Harm Your Health**​

Eventually we will be worrying about our health in general and in attempt to become supercentenarians.
There mTOR is a major player.
It is really a switch.
Mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) regulates cell proliferation, autophagy, and apoptosis by participating in multiple signaling pathways in the body.

**1\. cell proliferation.**
Happens when there is a plenty of nutrients.
Mostly good thing.
But since all is happy and growing, bad things also grow.

2\. **Autophagy and apoptosis.**
Happens when nutrients a scarce or none.
Autophagy and apoptosis are two different ways that cells can die.
Autophagy is a process where cells recycle their own components when they are deprived of nutrients.
Autophagy can also help the immune system by cleaning up toxins and infectious agents.

Apoptosis is used to rid the body of cells that have been damaged beyond repair.
Apoptosis also plays a role in preventing cancer.
\==================================

Before we even get to
cell proliferation, autophagy, and apoptosis
we should note that excessive LA Linoleic Acid
prevents proper functioning of all three above
but especially Autophagy.
Linoleic acid influence autophagy by affecting cardiolipin.

Therefore, even before we start talking about becoming supercentenarian,
getting rid of excessive LA is a first priority.

\=============
That takes a lot of energy, especially mental energy.

To get that energy, it would not hurt to have a best we are able to, cardiovascular system.
Good blood suply, clean veins, arteries and all else.
I am talking about
good levels of Nitric Oxide.

\=======
I am chewing on the third bottle of NO2U.
Started August 15, so two months and a week.
Previously I was walking daily 5 miles.
Now able to do half of that distance running.
I think it works.

////////////////

### JanSz — 2023-10-21T22:58:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-organic.28267/post-327463>

My doctor has this chart in his office.
I have recalculated that chart, trying to tease out numbers.
I am 5'8" = 68", 83.4 yo
I am weighting now 150# and have body fat 14% .
My body fat lately varies 13% to 15%
From the right chart it looks like
my minimum weight should not be less than 121# or more than 163#, average 142#
I really hate to be hungry, and looking for a round numbers as my excuse,
when thinking about the benefits of following the mTOR project, my weight should be variable 145#-155#
If I can manage, I will attempt to move the above down to 140#-150#.
Fasting insulin 2-5
Not sure about the time.
Historically, naturally speaking about situation on latitude where I am from by my mom and dad, it should be about 4/12.
4 months of good time
8 months of scarce food time of variable intensity.
but, if I am not mistaken (month has about 4 weeks), so
**one month of abundance following by two months of scarce time** should also be acceptable.

My 20000 years ago grandma and grandpa are from about the same latitude that I am now.
Grandma's place was about where Tbilisi Georgia is now.

### JanSz — 2023-10-22T01:46:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-organic.28267/post-327465>

When being on mTOR program,
one is of either of anabolic (building) mind
or catabolic mind (starvation)
Amount of leucine in the consumed food is of great concern.
The list below does not have to be eaten at the times given.
It is just idea how to eat 2000 calories a day and make body think that it is staring, so loose weight, but hopefully prevent hunger pains.

[**2000 calories meal plan low in leucine**](https://www.bing.com/search?q=2000%20calories%20meal%20plan%20low%20in%20leucine&form=M00060), arginine and sugar
Here’s a sample meal plan:

-   **Breakfast**: Oatmeal with banana, walnuts, and cinnamon
    -   Oatmeal: 40g (dry weight)
    -   Banana: 120g (one medium banana)
    -   Walnuts: 30g
    -   Cinnamon: 2g
-   **AM Snack**: Blueberries with nonfat plain Greek yogurt
    -   Blueberries: 150g
    -   Greek yogurt: 200g
-   **Lunch**: Zucchini noodles with quick turkey bolognese
    -   Zucchini: 200g (for noodles)
    -   Ground turkey: 100g
-   **PM Snack**: Apple slices with almond butter
    -   Apple: 150g (one medium apple)
    -   Almond butter: 32g (two tablespoons)
-   **Dinner**: Grilled salmon with roasted asparagus
    -   Salmon: 154g (one medium fillet)
    -   Asparagus: 134g (about six spears)
-   **Evening Snack**: Frozen chocolate-banana bites
    -   Banana: 120g (one medium banana)
    -   Dark chocolate: 20g

////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

[**2000 calories meal plan high in leucine**](https://www.bing.com/search?q=2000%20calories%20meal%20plan%20low%20in%20leucine&form=M00060)
selection of foods. Here’s a sample meal plan:

Creating a 2000 calorie meal plan with high leucine content requires careful selection of foods. Here’s a sample meal plan:

**Breakfast**:

-   [**Eggs**: 2 large eggs (100g) - 155 calories, 1.4g leucine](https://www.verywellfit.com/1-week-2000-calorie-meal-plan-ideas-recipes-and-prep-6362068)
-   [**Greek Yogurt**: 1 cup (200g) - 150 calories, 2.3g leucine](https://www.verywellfit.com/1-week-2000-calorie-meal-plan-ideas-recipes-and-prep-6362068)

**Mid-Morning Snack**:

-   [**Almonds**: 1 ounce (28g) - 164 calories, 0.8g leucine](https://www.verywellfit.com/1-week-2000-calorie-meal-plan-ideas-recipes-and-prep-6362068)

**Lunch**:

-   [**Chicken Breast**: 1 roasted breast (200g) - 335 calories, 3.6g leucine](https://www.verywellfit.com/1-week-2000-calorie-meal-plan-ideas-recipes-and-prep-6362068)
-   [**Brown Rice**: 1 cup cooked (195g) - 216 calories, 0.3g leucine](https://www.verywellfit.com/1-week-2000-calorie-meal-plan-ideas-recipes-and-prep-6362068)

**Mid-Afternoon Snack**:

-   [**Cottage Cheese**: 1 cup (226g) - 206 calories, 2.7g leucine](https://www.verywellfit.com/1-week-2000-calorie-meal-plan-ideas-recipes-and-prep-6362068)

**Dinner**:

-   [**Salmon**: Half fillet (154g) - 367 calories, 3.0g leucine](https://www.verywellfit.com/1-week-2000-calorie-meal-plan-ideas-recipes-and-prep-6362068)
-   [**Quinoa**: 1 cup cooked (185g) - 222 calories, 0.5g leucine](https://www.verywellfit.com/1-week-2000-calorie-meal-plan-ideas-recipes-and-prep-6362068)

**Before Bed**:

-   [**Milk**: 1 cup (244g) - 103 calories, 0.9g leucine](https://www.verywellfit.com/1-week-2000-calorie-meal-plan-ideas-recipes-and-prep-6362068)

### JanSz — 2023-10-25T01:46:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lets-talk-organic.28267/post-327524>

Production of Oxidised Linoleic Acid Metabolites (OXLAMs):
OXLAMs formed from LA activate NF-kB and increase proinflammatory cytokines, endothelial adhesion molecules, as well as chemokines, all of which are **paramount in the formation of atherosclerosis.
..**

### Espresso — 2023-10-24T14:58:19+0300

Fitting heat sinks with double sided tape pretty misses the point. In order to get the heat out, one would need to have direct contact with the heat source. Might as well just use blocks of wood to stiffen up the panel.

### RitaC — 2023-10-28T17:11:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-light.28269/post-327608>

> [Anthony Smith said:](/goto/post?id=327472)
>
> They both look nice, I have not used either but from a practical point of view the larger coverage area of the Inferno for daily maintenance/treatment would to me appear to be a better approach. I have two 850nm NIR lights I use that give good coverage and results I am happy with. NIR security lights are 850nm, I payed less than $200 for the two lights I have and I added a UVA light.

What UVA light did you add, why, and how do you use it?

### Anthony Smith — 2023-10-29T23:01:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/infra-red-light.28269/post-327649>

> [RitaC said:](/goto/post?id=327608)
>
> What UVA light did you add, why, and how do you use it?

I got the one Jack uses, it is offered in 30Watt or 60Watt I ordered the 60Watt but when it came it says 100Watt on the back. I later found out Jack’s is the 30AMP so I use mine a few feet away. This light has no ground and the electric field from it is significant so I recommend grounding the light. I use 2 850nm NIR lights first especially when indoors looking at a screen and add the UVA light during the day. This set up is behind and above my screen. Each NIR light is equivalent to a 500Watt quartz halogen work light, but being NIR all you see is a gentle glow.

I like the 2 NIR light set up because when using for healing I can turn them toward each other and treat front and back at the same time - shoulder, knee etc. I would be much closer and leave the UVA light off when doing that. I will use the NIR by itself BUT never use the UVA without the NIR. Others may disagree but this what I have and do, it works for me for my wife and I for pain relief and healing.

Total set up: 2 NIR lights, UVA light and stand (from HomeDepot) about $250.
