# Thread: Sprinting, Jumping, Throwing as a cold adapted mammal - Trying To Make Sense of My Phenotype
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 82
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sfast — 2023-11-06T10:32:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-327905>

Hi,

this is a longer musing. I hope there is something useful for you

A Personal Fitness Phenotype
B I Do Well in the Cold
C How I Do My CT
D Effect of CT On My Fitness
E A Fitness Protocol for the Cold Adapted Mammal
F Sense Making

**A Personal Fitness Phenotype**

I seem to have a strange fitness phenotype:

1\. I am exceptionally explosive in the upper body and very explosive in the lower body.
2\. I am very strong in my upper body and above average in my lower body.
3\. I have below average muscular stamina, especially when it comes to static demands (like holding a push-up half way down)
4\. I have below average endurance when the demands are in the moderate time frame (20-40 min). When I was younger and did boxing, my best was a measly 3.73 miles (6km) in 24 min (on the second). It is a good performance for an average person, but I was training multiple times per day. So, not very good considering my training background.

So, even without any biopsies, I feel confident in saying that I have naturally high fast twitch fiber content and low slow twitch fiber content. So far, so boring.

But then there is a strange outlier in my endurance profile: Repeated explosive contractions with moderate rest over a long period of time. Examples:

1\. I could jump 5-stair-jumps (at once) for 1 hour straight. Jump, reset, jump, reset etc. One jump every 6-10 seconds.
2\. I could wrestle for an almost an hour straight with alternating training partners (I kept being on the mat, the partners took their turns on me)
3\. I could chop wood for days without regular practice (from sun rise to sun down), even when I could even hold a cup in the evening because of fatigue.
4\. When I trained indoor rowing, I got to a pretty high level (C2, 15-20x 100m intervals per session, below 14s on each interval)

**B I Do Well in the Cold**
For some reason, I always had a strong inclination to reject warm clothing. It started as a normal child thing (at least, this is what I observed: We all went out with no jacket), it turned into some strange "I am too tough" when I grew older and turned into good tolerance of intense CT (35min, 32°F, no shivering, no problem with direct ice to skin contact, no need to deliberately heat myself up).

When I do my sprints, I don't feel that I need extensive warm up or clothing. Even in freezing temperature, I just go out in shorts and hoodie, do a couple of slower sprints and feel springy already. I neither feel warm, nor cold. It is more that I feel decoupled from temperature. After my workout, I feel a steady rise in internal heat, but not to a point of discomfort.

**C How I Do My CT**
I am not meticulous about planning it. I just have my barrel outside and increase my time in the cold. I adapt to the temperature fluctuations by feel. But I don't shy away from ice, nor do I put on any clothes except a beanie in case of wind chill. It took one season, but now I don't get cold burn, even with direct ice to skin contact for 30 min.

A strange side effect seems to be that I can't get sun burn any more. When my skin seems to be burned, I turn brown the next day instead of red. I darken up very quickly (within one day) and whiten back also quickly.

**D Effect of CT On My Fitness**

When I do my CT, my work capacity increases considerably. I think the reason is that I treat my CT as training, while other athletes (both professional and recreational) treat it just as icing on the cake. This is very strange, since the intensity of your bodies reaction is high and very warranted. Example: I think Peter Attia is responsible for the new wave of Zone 2 cardio in the new school of longevity. So, many people are now doing their long runs for their mitochondria. But I never read, aside from [@Barry](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/717/), about the power of intimately coupling cold and endurance. Why not? The mitochondrial stimulus by cold, especially if you are healthy and don't need to be that careful is immense.

However, the interesting thing about me increasing my cold adaptation is that it seems to deepen my phenotype. I seem to get overall benefits in terms of energy metabolism, stamina and endurance without any downsides regarding strength and explosiveness. But I have the feeling that repeated explosive bouts almost can't fatigue me, when I have a certain level of cold adaptation. It almost feels as if my fast twitch fibers can bypass glycolysis, and I am just running on creatine phosphate and fat.

I think _distributed_ exercise in combination with cold adaptations offers a unique advantage compared to more traditional approaches. If you study Rich Fronings way of training, you'll see the overlap (especially in the earlier phases of his career). No wonder, that he had such a high VO2max. A lot of shorter workouts, spread throughout the days, never shying away from cold or being outside, some form of intermittent fasting (not sure if he still does this) etc.

**E A Fitness Protocol for the Cold Adapted Mammal**
Currently, I am working a lot and have a young daughter. Therefore, I can't live a self-optimised life as I could a couple of years ago. Also, I am almost 40, therefore I move my focus more to longevity, though I subscribe to Peter Attia's notion that you need to be as fit as possible when young to increase your health span (which was already stated by Greg Glassman more than a decade ago, by the way).

\- Early Morning: Start with CT to pre-cool (directly after brushing teeth) first thing in the morning. Slow run with my dog in a tank-top, sometimes with beanie to fight the windchill (I shave my head). The run is 0min. Then I do my strength training, almost full body split. 70-80% of 1RM, sticking to Prilepin's Chart. Sometimes, do a couple more sets by feel. Strength training is 0min most of the time <20min
\- During the first 5-6 hours of my working day: Every 40-60 min I have a short exercise snack. Sometimes, I do 20-30 burpees + ring rows, sometimes dumbbell snatches for 2-3mins etc. Two times a week I do mobility/stability instead (e.g. KneesOverToesGuy stuff). I spread my training load throughout the day and also limit the amount of uninterrupted sitting. I don't do well on a standing desk.
\- Noon: 10-30 mins of either medium intensity circuit training (e.g. Pushup, Row, Squat), moderate intensity continuous stuff like EMOM or throwing a light slam ball (45lbs) or more stability/mobility. A long walk with my dog, always with shorts, sometimes just T-shirt as well.
\- Noon (just on wednesdays and saturdays): Sprinting (50m sprint, slow 50m walk) for 10-15min, stair jumps + plyos 5-10 mins or medicine ball throws.
\- Afternoon: I try to get up every 30-60 mins and do some light mobility stuff that gets my heart going.

**F Sense Making**

\- _Hypothesis:_ If you have some type of sickness, distributing the training load is a way to manage fatigue, allow for a higher training volume and therefore increase desirable training adaptation. Long sessions are draining, both physically and mentally. But just 10-20 min of light jogging in the morning is much more doable. No need to hit the gym for 90 mins. Just do 20 min of moderately intense callisthenics. If you connect your short training sessions to transition times (like morning routine, lunch break, end of work), the volume can add up without any need to make profound chances to your schedule or tanking your system. Just start very light: 10 min of brisk walking in the morning, a couple of minutes of non-exhaustive wall-pushups, planks, deadhangs during lunch break, just one bout of 20-30s of flight of stairs.
\- _Hypothesis:_ There is magic to the combination of cold stimulus and blue collar work. My athletic training (sprinting, jumping, throwing) is done in a blue collar type of programming. Instead of taking long rest, I spread the bouts more evenly throughout my training session. I do shorter sprints, but do shorter breaks. I jump for 10 minutes, but leave 6-10s of space between each jump. There are now sturdier people in the world than physical workers that expose themselves to cold simultaneously. (Francis Ngannou btw. worked in sand mines as a child and told that during the rain session you have to keep moving to manage the cold. I think his strong physique has something to do with this combination) I think this type of training. Imitating repeated but distributed efforts for longer periods of time in the cold does something different to your body than traditional training.
\- _Hypothesis:_ Cold somehow mitigates the downsides of training while not hindering training adaptations. To me, it seems that being in cold or even pre-cooling mitigates training stress without hindering the training adaptations. Studies on the problematic effects of cold on strength training adaptations are thinking of cold as something that you add to your strength training. I think it should be the other way around: Training is something that can be added to your cold exposure.
\- _Hypothesis:_ The deep adaptations of cold both acute and chronic are putting your body in a unique state that can and should be exploited. I don't understand a lot of what Dr. Kruse is teaching. But it stuck to me that the physiology of a cold-adapted mammal works differently. My personal experience confirms him. I am a different species when I am cold adapted _and_ I am a different species the moment I switch to a cold environment.

### Jack Kruse — 2023-11-07T18:36:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-327986>

Get your haplotype and your Fitzpatrick skin type and add that to the mix. Then add in the non visual photoreceptor stimulus in the mix and then the POMC status and then add in the redox labs look and everything makes sense.

### sfast — 2023-11-08T12:32:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328022>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=327986)
>
> Fitzpatrick skin type

This is actually very tricky to me. I get very pale in the winter. But when the summer arrives, I get tan very quickly. So, before CT I was type II-III, I think. Now, it looks to me that I am something like a type II/type IV hybrid, while almost never have a burn. Even my eyes reflect that: I have both green and brown spots. Sometimes, they look almost golden.

I'd need to find my exact haplo type. My ancestry is ~25% Celtic, 25% Eastern European, 50%, so I'd be very surprised my haplo type is different from being an ice bear.  There is an additional caveat to my heritage, since my family was subject to harsh selection pressures during the Soviet Union. All my ancestors are in some way hardier than the other Germans which might be in part to selection pressures and in part due to epigenetic changes.

This is the strangest effect of CT to me: It somehow made me a hybrid in many aspects of my physical being. Right now, purely in a beneficial sense. (and CT improved my heat tolerance in summer _a lot_)

I need to find some possiblity to test my redox potential in my area. This would be very interesting.

I think my core object of research is how to move to the next level. I hacked back my immune system by adopting a paleo-inspired lifestyle after it was shot because of mould in my flat. CT started as a nice training tool in my toolbox. But it completely changed my physique (and in part my mind) when I reached a deeper adaptation level.

There are many stories about native people having seemingly supernatural abilities. I experienced profound changes with CT after a threshold of adaptation (I went from 3 min in 50-55°F with huffing, puffing and shivering to 35 mins in 32°F with little effect to my breathing and manifest stress response, no shivering and no need to help with warm up (I just went out and cooked dinner which was enough to heat up my body)).

I think the tremendous work of you, [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) , has the priority. But what after healing?

There is an intimate connection to the question I asked in the other thread "How far can we take CT and still get life-relevant adaptations, rather than just improving our ability to withstand cold?" and the reasons I presented my experience and musings in this thread: Many people are satisfied with mediocre results because they come from a bad place. Perfectly understandable and not only rational but even spiritual sound. If you started very sick there are plenty of good reasons to be very happy if you arrive to a place with no pain or even a bit of thriving. But to quote the seer from Vikings: "If you would know what the gods have planned for you, you would be dancing naked at the beach."

The natural question then is, what is the _actual_ realistic expectation for a person. WAY higher than commonly believed, I think.

### JanSz — 2023-11-08T18:21:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328035>

Cold Thermogenesis (CT) has been associated with several health benefits that could potentially contribute to longevity:

**Metabolism and Weight Management:** CT stimulates the body’s metabolism, leading to increased energy production and calorie burning. It also promotes the burning of white fat and the increase of brown fat, which can help with weight management.

**Brain Health:** CT may have benefits for brain health. The breakdown of brain synapses, or degeneration, is a normal side effect of aging.

**Immunity:** Regular exposure to cold environments may boost immunity.

**Sleep:** CT has been associated with improved sleep.

**Inflammation:** CT stimulates the release of norepinephrine, which can inhibit inflammation pathways. Lower inflammation is a predictor for survival and cognitive capabilities.

**Longevity-Associated Molecules:** Cold exposure has been shown to stimulate brown fat and **inhibit mTOR** pathways, both associated with increasing longevity-associated molecules.

### sfast — 2023-11-09T08:42:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328066>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=328035)
>
> Cold Thermogenesis (CT) has been associated with several health benefits that could potentially contribute to longevity:
>
> **Metabolism and Weight Management:** CT stimulates the body’s metabolism, leading to increased energy production and calorie burning. It also promotes the burning of white fat and the increase of brown fat, which can help with weight management.
>
> **Brain Health:** CT may have benefits for brain health. The breakdown of brain synapses, or degeneration, is a normal side effect of aging.
>
> **Immunity:** Regular exposure to cold environments may boost immunity.
>
> **Sleep:** CT has been associated with improved sleep.
>
> **Inflammation:** CT stimulates the release of norepinephrine, which can inhibit inflammation pathways. Lower inflammation is a predictor for survival and cognitive capabilities.
>
> **Longevity-Associated Molecules:** Cold exposure has been shown to stimulate brown fat and **inhibit mTOR** pathways, both associated with increasing longevity-associated molecules.

I know the research to some degree. Can confirm from an anecdotal standpoint all of the above from feel.

I think there is research warranted for being somewhat at 80-90% of the limits of cold adaptation. Just think about that: Just like the _average_ Westerner cannot jog for more than a couple of minutes, the average person cannot even withstand a couple of seconds of ice water. However, being able to run a marathon is possible for not just for the average person but even for people challenged with all kinds of illnesses.

When I started CT, I stopped getting sick. I stopped doing it intensively when my wife got pregnant and got sick. It is just plain simple to me: CT = never getting sick, no CT = getting infections like anyone else.

I wonder how the physiology books would have to change if it was normal to be able to get into ice water for an hour without shivering.

### sfast — 2023-11-11T13:37:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328151>

It is raining wet dogs as we say in Germany. 45°F. Time to go out and do some sprint training and throw the medicine ball. The stress response is altered when we face the difficult willingly.

Also: Strength, Sprints, Throwing - all can and should be (as part of a general fitness program) with short, frequent and multiple bursts. Instead of doing 4x10 reps, I suggest doing 8x5 with the same weight and half of the rest period. Energy usage shifts to CP + Fat and away from glycogen, which improves exercise tolerance. Important for people who train a lot or can't train a lot because of sickness.

### John Warner — 2023-11-11T18:02:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328178>

I do MovNat and became stronger, I've done CT for almost 4 years, always did centralized way. Ice then workout, felt good. Then this year CT before and sometimes after = bigger effect. ( Sometimes after depending on home or in the woods). N =1.

### sfast — 2023-11-13T08:54:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328223>

[@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) US is something else.

[@John Warner](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/25255/) I don't think it is that clear cut, either. The majority of research indicates that CT after strength is not the best way. However, they isolate when live is integrated. MovNat invokes adaptations through way more than just increased muscle and if you are doing enough the sum of CT and training is higher even if CT is done after.

I test a hypotheses: If you activate AMPK before strength training, something magical seem to happen. Way higher (acute) response:

\- [http://suppversity.blogspot.de/2014/07/concomitant-training-cardio-before-or.html](http://suppversity.blogspot.de/2014/07/concomitant-training-cardio-before-or.html)
\- [http://suppversity.blogspot.de/2016/04/cardio-can-boost-your-gains-do-it.html](http://suppversity.blogspot.de/2016/04/cardio-can-boost-your-gains-do-it.html)
\- [https://suppversity.blogspot.com/2011/11/buiding-bigger-engine-resistance-after.html](https://suppversity.blogspot.com/2011/11/buiding-bigger-engine-resistance-after.html)

Also: For me, it is just practical. I do a big chunk of my training and all of my CT in the morning. CT can't be last because then I don't perform at my best at my work. CT can't be in between. So, it has to be first.

However, it is a good fit: CT + (short) run is an awesome combination to load AMPK. Zone 2 + higher intensity at the end seems also a pretty good combo to increase all kinds of adaptations I am after.

\--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Today's CT was 10min @38°F + 30min in air afterwards with tank top and short shorts. It took me a while to get into the groove with strength training. I think this will improve considerably when enter the deeper layer of cold adaptation and my running ability improves (I re-introduce Zone 2 after more than a decade of just crossfitesque HIIT), so I can have a higher energy turn over for longer.

### sfast — 2023-11-13T08:56:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328224>

One additional thought: CT before and training in cold a two VERY different things. CT before introduces the need to warm up and adjust. If I just go into the cold my need for warm-up seems to even decrease.

I'd love to see a study that looks at strongman type strength training with medium loads in the cold.

### sfast — 2023-11-13T10:47:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328225>

This is by the way the 30 year old me:

Note that I didn't have any hypertrophy specific training in my program. It was purely sets below 3 reps + high to very high intensity endurance training + athletics (mainly sprints + throwing). This form is the result of specific adaptations to a training focussed on athleticism and not any bodybuilding. This I used my flat back then with no heat + windchill (I sat exactly between an open window and the door to the outside) for CT (6-8 hours in 50-60°F temperature + whatever the windchill added). I started to do one training session early in the morning with this exact clothing style in the low 20s°F. My training adaptations back then were accelerated to such a degree that I ripped one head of my left gastrocnemius. Explosive Power training in cold was a cheat code for me.

I am currently reading the fasted training thread by [@Barry](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/717/) . I feel that the focus on fasted ultra endurance is misguided on the research side. Yes, the data point of ultra long distance, fasted endurance is essential. But the theoretical understanding on longevity and performance can't be achieved by a narrow perspective.

Example: My family heritage is a certain branch of Germans that migrated to Russia (what is today Ukraine) were then deported back to Germany by the Nazis and after the end of WWII were deported to Siberia and Kyrgyzstan to finally re-immigrate to Germany in the late 70s. The level of hardiness and the physical feats that they pulled off are insane by today's standard.

I am very soft compared to my older relatives that lived through that environment. But still, when I was younger (puberty) it was _normal_ for me to get drunk (barely not vomiting), partying all night and then jog back home at 5am for 10 miles with little clothing on. I am not telling this to brag. To repeat myself: This is nothing compared to what was normal and still is normal in parts of the world.

I'd bet my left pinky that you'd have got some insane values if you tested my grandparents (or all the other workers) back then.

You never know what is the actual nature of us humans if you don't know what is the limit. You don't know what true happiness and meaning is unless you had TRUE peak experiences. And you don't know what health is if you never experienced your peak fitness.

Now, I am transitioning from a performance focussed approach towards longevity and I guess a more calm-spiritual way because it is more compatible with me being a husband/father/older (approaching 40). So, I think, I will settle within a body with 15-20 pounds less muscle than in the picture, a somewhat higher bodyfat percentage (my daughter is 1y/o. So, I still have a six-pack, but I am way fatter now) and less tightness that might be good for explosive power but not tolerable when aging.

### sfast — 2023-11-13T15:34:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328229>

I am currently at page 67 of [@Barry](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/717/) 's Fasted State Training thread. The missing piece of moving the understanding forward is laid out in E.O. Wilson's _Consilience of Knowledge_. This book is extremely underrated and will be for some time, since science it caged in the university walls, real world is driven away from the scientific method and the general culture (this is not a western phenomenon!) is in decay.(1) It is as if our collective corpus callosum is broken.

The problem of decay shows first when Barry missed basic tools, even though he is exceptionally smart and educated. But before it was present by his focus on ultra endurance and dismissal of other data points as irrelevant, instead of created the proper relationship. An example is Rich Fronings bizarre VO2max (in the mid to high 70s). It is bizarre because he does a lot of explosive stuff. I wouldn't be surprised if his within-day-deficit would put him in the same realm of Barry's fasted and long sessions. He was famous for not eating all day during his training...

One of my main objects of interests is tied to this: Thinking tools and the biological/psychological environment of thinking (properly). The science we believe, for example, is done by people who are addicted to their smartphones, see 95% artificial blue light and have dying mitochondria. So, no wonder why it is often abysmal. But the grounding of any person's thinking depends on way more than most people offer themselves.

I feel that the knowledge could've been developed much further by including more data points. E.G. connecting each one if they share a common pattern.

For context: I am writing this from a point of great respect for Barry. I am very grateful for this thread, because there are a lot of gems buried in it. I try to push my own understanding further.

(1): Why is this important? Because the very way we are able to (re-)construct reality isn't independent of the ancestral knowledge buried in culture. As Nietzsche said (paraphrasing): Rationality (and rationalism!) was made possible by the domestication of the European mind by the church (mostly catholic). We still feast on the dead corps of this domestication. Just look at the ugliness of the vast majority of cultural expression today.

### John Warner — 2023-11-13T16:47:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328235>

I used to try and test everything, I still do blood work and Scan when needed( not near as often as central med. wants ) from prior cancer. I go by feel now I still change things up and try different methods,CT, Time of day workout, food right after or wait a bit. I think most studies on exercise are prob Blue lit gym and circadian lifestyle are not considered. MovNat has helped me be stronger and mobile, I'm Irish, Norwegian, German. never was able to pack on muscle, fine with that now, I took the Irish for size not the Norwegian. I do better in the Cold for most everything, Summers are getting rough on me, maybe haplotype does matter on CT before and after? Northern European may not matter when doin CT, unlike a coupled Haplotype? think I've heard or read that somewhere. goin to go look for that. MovNat har 1/2 days of week then LIGHT MovNat rest of week. Recovery more Important after 50. CT most days 50 degrees, started this fall goin deeper, deeper I go old injuries get sore after.

### JanSz — 2023-11-13T17:31:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328244>

Some of my observations:
1.Supercentenarians are rather short.
I asked Jack why around us these days, sons are usually taller that their fathers or grandfathers.
So far, no answer. But I am still curious.
I suspect that that growth has some correlation with Linoleic Acid (seed oils).
Afterall, it speeds up growth of farmed animals.
Chicken from egg to slaughter 35 days, rather than previously many months.
If one wait to a day 36, chicks would die, likely from insanely sick cardiovascular system.

2\. They do eat meat, but much less.

This may be highly important, since in the last 150 years we are highly overloaded with LA, Linoleic Acid (Omega-6) from those meats.
Not only in America, everywhere. Normally farmed, organic, even venison (when bought at the store). Soon, even wild seafood will get it or may already have it.
That causes inflammation.

3\. Supercentenarians are not hungry; they eat a lot of (low glycemic) carbohydrates (so rather high volumes).
(Volumes of food, mouth and intestines are getting its workout).

> [sfast said:](/goto/post?id=328229)
>
> I am currently at page 67 of [@Barry](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/717/) 's Fasted State Training thread. The missing piece of moving the understanding forward is laid out in E.O. Wilson's _Consilience of Knowledge_. This book is extremely underrated and will be for some time, since science it caged in the university walls, real world is driven away from the scientific method and the general culture (this is not a western phenomenon!) is in decay.(1) It is as if our collective corpus callosum is broken.
>
> The problem of decay shows first when Barry missed basic tools, even though he is exceptionally smart and educated. But before it was present by his focus on ultra endurance and dismissal of other data points as irrelevant, instead of created the proper relationship. An example is Rich Fronings bizarre VO2max (in the mid to high 70s). It is bizarre because he does a lot of explosive stuff. I wouldn't be surprised if his within-day-deficit would put him in the same realm of Barry's fasted and long sessions. He was famous for not eating all day during his training...
>
> One of my main objects of interests is tied to this: Thinking tools and the biological/psychological environment of thinking (properly). The science we believe, for example, is done by people who are addicted to their smartphones, see 95% artificial blue light and have dying mitochondria. So, no wonder why it is often abysmal. But the grounding of any person's thinking depends on way more than most people offer themselves.
>
> I feel that the knowledge could've been developed much further by including more data points. E.G. connecting each one if they share a common pattern.
>
> For context: I am writing this from a point of great respect for Barry. I am very grateful for this thread, because there are a lot of gems buried in it. I try to push my own understanding further.
>
> (1): Why is this important? Because the very way we are able to (re-)construct reality isn't independent of the ancestral knowledge buried in culture. As Nietzsche said (paraphrasing): Rationality (and rationalism!) was made possible by the domestication of the European mind by the church (mostly catholic). We still feast on the dead corps of this domestication. Just look at the ugliness of the vast majority of cultural expression today.

Interesting that my thread is still of interest, glad its giving you the stimulus [@sfast](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/15697/).

CT has its merits but I still don't think its the be all and end all to super human performance. If it was, then we would see world class athletes doing it. In addition, as you probably are aware, you need to be fully fat adapted also. This is a long journey, 24-36months.

However, remember, that the 100m sprint champion and the marathon champion, have both probably never sat for regular periods in an ice bath and ate an Epi-Paleo diet. Its always important to scale your achievements and abilities. Guys like Jack and Wim Hof have taken CT to the extreme, but neither of them have performed at any sort of world class level in any particular sport. I was fully fat adapted and also using CT, but I wasn't winning the world Ultra championships. Other guys, that eat a high carb diet and live in a warm climate were.

I'm not ruling it out, I'm simply saying that we still haven't gotten proof of any athlete using CT/Fat Adaptation//Photoreceptors/Redox/etc etc that is performing at a world class level. One of the reasons I think we never will, is that in order to optimize all these things, you need to be living sort of like a monk. Real life ain't like that for most of us.

Which is why I spend more time now on just trying to stay healthy and battle against the agenda that our rulers want us to follow. More importantly, I have young kids that need to be steered clear of all the madness, and that is a challenge in itself.

[According to a study, body fat percentage is a better predictor of diabetes risk than body mass index (BMI)](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/321567) [1](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/321567).
[**People with a body fat percentage of 20-35% may be at risk of developing diabetes**](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/321567) [1](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/321567)

\=================================================================

### [Diabetes: Body fat percentage, not BMI, predicts risk](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/321567)

A new study suggests that the standard body mass index cannot accurately predict someone's risk of developing type 2 diabetes or prediabetes.

 www.medicalnewstoday.com

### Diabetes: Body fat percentage, not BMI, predicts risk​

The results of a new study urge healthcare providers to account for body fat distribution, in addition to body mass index, in their decision to screen people for diabetes and prediabetes.
Share on PinterestOur standard measurements of what constitutes a healthy weight may need revising, suggests a new study.
Body mass index ([BMI](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/info/obesity/what-is-bmi.php)) is a traditional measurement that divides a person’s weight by their height to find out whether they have a healthy weight.

However, increasing amounts of studies have been questioning its usefulness and accuracy as an indicator of cardiometabolic health.
The [distribution of fat](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/321076.php), rather than the total amount, these studies suggest, may give us more clues about the risk of conditions such as [insulin resistance](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/305567.php), [high blood pressure](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/159283.php), [heart disease](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/237191.php), and even [cancer](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/info/cancer-oncology/).
For instance, a recent study drew attention to the “[overfat pandemic](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/318647.php)” that is spreading across the United States. The researchers used the term overfat to describe the accumulation of fat around certain parts of the body, which, the scientists point out, is not accounted for by BMI.
In fact, a low BMI may be misleading. Belly fat is particularly harmful, research shows, with some showing that it can raise the risk of [diabetes](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/info/diabetes/) and heart disease while [others](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/321301.php) reveal the mechanisms behind this association.
Now, a [new study](http://bmjopen.bmj.com/content/8/4/e019200#block-system-main) — which has been published in the journal _BMJ Open_ — adds to this growing body of research, as researchers from the University of Florida (UF) in Gainesville discover that people with a normal BMI but high body fat are more prone to prediabetes or diabetes, when compared with people deemed overweight according to their BMI but have a lower body fat percentage.
Body fat percentage calculates the proportion of a person’s fat mass to their lean muscle mass.

### High body fat, not BMI, predicts diabetes​

For this study, the scientists — led by Ara Jo, Ph.D., a clinical assistant professor in the Department of Health Services Research, Management and Policy at UF — examined data available from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey.
This survey was carried out by the National Center for Health Statistics of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), and it used interviews, as well as physical and laboratory tests, to examine the health of adults aged 40 and above between 1999 and 2006.
Jo and her team focused on adults who had never been diagnosed with [type 2 diabetes](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/info/diabetes/type2diabetes.php), and they used a scanning technique called [dual-energy X-ray absorptiometryTrusted Source](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5410155/) — which is the most accurate technique available — to measure body fat percentage.
**They used the guidelines issued by the American Association of Clinical Endocrinologists and the American College of Endocrinology to estimate what counts as high body fat for men and for women.**
According to these guidelines, having a percentage of body fat of 25 and above is considered high for men, and 35 percent is considered high body fat for women.
**Based on these measurements, the analysis revealed that 13.5 percent of people with a normal BMI and a high body fat percentage had prediabetes or diabetes, compared with only 10.5 percent of those deemed “overweight” by their BMI but who had low body fat.**
“This high body fat percent link to abnormal blood glucose,” explains senior study author Arch G. Mainous III, the chair of the UF Department of Health Services Research, Management and Policy, “holds up even when we control for things like age, sex, race/ethnicity, family history of diabetes, vigorous-intensity exercise, and muscle strengthening activities.”

### Normal BMI not a measure of health​

“Typically, normal BMI has been perceived as healthy,” explains Jo, “so people with normal BMI have been neglected in several preventive care guidelines.”
“Yet, normal BMI does not necessarily mean healthy body composition,” she cautions. Mainous echoes the same thoughts, saying, “Evidence has been mounting that BMI may not be the best measure of body fat for a variety of groups like individuals who are sedentary or older women.”

> “This study provides more support for this idea of skinny fat and shows how percent body fat is more important in identifying individuals with prediabetes than BMI.”
> Arch G. Mainous III

“It also alerts us,” he adds, “to consider ways of better identifying individuals with elevated body fat and incorporating it into clinical practice.”
“We hope these findings may inspire physicians and other health professionals to look more closely at the normal BMI population and provide preventive care on time for those who are at risk of developing diabetes,” concludes Jo.

-   [Diabetes](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/categories/diabetes)
-   [Type 2](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/diabetes/type-2)
-   [Public Health](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/categories/public-health)
-   [Obesity / Weight Loss / Fitness](https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/categories/fitness-obesity)

Many thanks for your reply!

> CT has its merits but I still don't think its the be all and end all to super human performance.

100% agreed, that it is not the ultima ratio for super human performance. However, I think the effects are quite pronounced. Cold gets a thumbs down after strength exercise. I thought the other way around: If such a low cold stimulus used in these studies is strong enough to blunt adaptations, how powerful of a stimulus per se is cold then?

My hypothesis is that we didn't figure out how to properly include cold adaptation into an overall training regime. There are parallels to Zone 2 cardio: In the strength world, it was thought to be damaging. Crossfit rejected it for a long time. But slowly people figured out how to properly dose and time it. Now, it is back (even in my regime and I was a high intensity zealot!) and a staple in many fields that formerly rejected it.

> If it was, then we would see world class athletes doing it.

I disagree.

1\. It is a fallacy on a formal level: If our world class athletes would do what is best, there wouldn't be any improvements in training/diet/recovery possible.
2\. There are gems like Kobe O'Brien, who took is whole life approach to a new level.
3\. I teach people methods of knowledge work. (Zettelkasten Method) To get the system fully working you need to invest in it for some time. It enhances your ability to deal with large and complex bodies of knowledge by a great extend. But here we are, reaping the benefits of genius level knowledge developed by inferior tools. The best are often in a meta-stable position and refrain from the risk of destabilisation that would be necessary to get to the next level.

Sadly, it will take some odd ball with amazing genetics to showcase the benefits of it.

> In addition, as you probably are aware, you need to be fully fat adapted also. This is a long journey, 24-36months.

I am aware of the hypothesis.  I don't buy it. I think that fat adaptation is part of metabolic flexibility which is the actual trait to maximize. At least, if you want to compete in complex sports. In the endurance world, the emphasis is on fat adaptation is higher for sure.

But my experience is that the effect of piecing everything together is that one can be almost carb independent. This was my main observation of my personal journey.

I was never able to ground the time frame of 2-3 years in evidence. Can you point me towards it?

> I was fully fat adapted and also using CT, but I wasn't winning the world Ultra championships. Other guys, that eat a high carb diet and live in a warm climate were.

Genetic freakery needs to be thrown into the mix, too.  But I'd like to take the chance and say: Great respect to your achievements. I hope some young gun will take the next step in the journey that progressed further.

> I'm not ruling it out, I'm simply saying that we still haven't gotten proof of any athlete using CT/Fat Adaptation//Photoreceptors/Redox/etc etc that is performing at a world class level. One of the reasons I think we never will, is that in order to optimize all these things, you need to be living sort of like a monk. Real life ain't like that for most of us.

I won't go that far that we will never see it. But it will take some time. But I think there are a lot of people who would be willing to take it that far. There are _many_ people who dedicate their lives to more obscure vocations.

> Which is why I spend more time now on just trying to stay healthy and battle against the agenda that our rulers want us to follow. More importantly, I have young kids that need to be steered clear of all the madness, and that is a challenge in itself.

This indeed a more important task (the same I have taken on and the reason why I am switching to training for longevity and life relevant performance). Much love and blessings to you and your family.

### sfast — 2023-11-18T12:13:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328400>

[@Barry](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/717/)

Additional idea: I think the high carb athletes are actually doing the very thing I think is the way: With high volume training, they tap into the fat metabolism also, though not to the extent a low-carb athlete is doing. But they marry both pathways to a higher total level.

I didn't exactly figure out how your training and eating schedule was, but when I trained on a lowcarb diet, I consumed 7000 kcal and more. (bodyweight back then was 105kg) I suspect that your intake was much lower. So, I'd think that you ate too little for the high training volume. My bodyweight was at 7-8% even though I drank heavy cream to all of my meals just to keep up with my cal.

I think the step forward would be not to follow the carb restrictions to keep a normally active person in the ketogenic state, but to utilise the higher carb tolerance for refeeds, regeneration etc. and still be in a ketogenic state for a long period of time.

"More useful would likely be to figure out how to change one's diet."

Beyond don't eat processed crap and focus on seasonal epi paleo, I won't be fearing one fat from the photosynthetic food chain and adopting a diet that ignores the influence of light, water, and magnetism. Sense of community, love, joy, purpose in life, avoiding accidents and infection, mitochondrial DNA inheritance etc ... all are factors in longevity.

Linking LA to supercentenarians doesn't pass the sniff test. Not everyone limiting LA in meat makes it to centenarian, let alone supercentenarian. Even the so called 5 blue zones aren't all semi vegetarian, and have different traditional diets. One link of the 5 blue zones may be that their water contains silica which removes aluminum - but that brings me back to **light** absorption and endogenous light emission, not LA.

Some supercentenarians in the general population may eat small amounts of meat, but traditional foods (before processed foods ) would have had naturally deuterium loaded foods from nature, including those that also contain LA. Maintaining the ingenious methods to get rid of _excess_ deuterium whilst maintaining _enough_ mass may be the clue. But they may also have a good maternal mitochondrial DNA inheritance, and the large majority of their lives to date would have been without cell towers and the current technology, and at a time when more people worked outside and the water supply wasn't fluoridated and chlorinated, **(water** \- hydrogen bonding networks) and the exposure to aluminum from birth or in utero wouldn't have been the same. Homes were probably different too, in the sense that the housing materials used for flooring and footwear used now block or blunt the earth's **magnetic field** signalling, which would normally be helping to maintain circadian rhythm, and possibly deuterium depletion during sleep, because darkness is when magnetism, not light, is the dominant circadian signal (Becker).

I think this is another example of the risks associated with trying to out supplement mother nature.

Taking a nitric oxide supplement to be able to run 5 miles instead of walking it, without considering whether my heart heteroplasmy could withstand that stress is not something I would consider, particularly at over 80 years old, and in a less than optimal environment. Doing a 5 mile walk barefoot on the beach with the waves lapping round my feet is a far safer choice (physically and epigenetically) than a 5 mile run in a built up area (or worse on a treadmill in a gym).

I would want to know why I needed a potassium supplement to avoid GERD or a large dose of chromium etc.

This is a post from Jack on a thread that I found really important for my n=1. He mentions IGF2.

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/building-a-childs-brain-begins-with-electrons.27475/#post-324111](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/building-a-childs-brain-begins-with-electrons.27475/#post-324111)

_"For example, the mouse Igf2 gene is expressed in a mouse only if it was inherited from the mouse's father."

"There is also a lot of data over the last sixty years that an “altered field” also causes the same symptoms common to many of these diseases as well as laid out by Robert Becker and Russ Addy. The field refers to the local environment that cells and the organism find themselves in. The field constantly changes as we move through the environment. This includes the nutrients available to us and the electromagnetic field around us."_

My father died of cancer in his early 70s, before all the current technology.

### [IGF2 gene: MedlinePlus Genetics](https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/gene/igf2/#conditions)

The IGF2 gene provides instructions for making a protein called insulin-like growth factor 2. Learn about this gene and related health conditions.

 medlineplus.gov

_"An increased amount of insulin-like growth factor 2 may stimulate the growth of tumor cells and prevent damaged cells from being destroyed."_

If the IGF2 rodent studies are done in humans to see if they bring down dementia risk, I won't be volunteering for the trial if there is an increased risk of cancer taking me out first.

### sfast — 2023-11-23T08:49:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328567>

[@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) Please do not hijack my thread.

\--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Yesterday, I tested accidentally my work capacity: I did stair jumps for 30 min non-stop (jump 5 stairs and walk back down, repeat) while talking to another person. I had no problems with neither explosive power nor endurance. This came as a surprise by me. But it fits to my hypothesis of CT increasing general work capacity with no interference with explosiveness.

On the level of training planing, I treat CT as no-impact endurance training. I think this is one of the main problems of CT. It is treated as a regeneration tool. But if you treat it as training for GPP you open yourself up to a pretty awesome method.

[@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) I think this might be a way to popularise CT and create a gateway to your work. Few people care about therapy, fewer people are willing to enter the realm of quantum thinking. But athletes are in my opinion easy to convince. And the protocol is simple, doesn't interfere with the rest of the training and will most likely improve work capacity, which can be even addictive to athletes (since many of them are sport addicted).

The protocol is simple: Start the day with CT. Start short and "warm". Increase time and reduce temperature with the conscious goal of reaching 30min in 40°F Water. Use that time for mental exercise. (I used ice bathing as biofeedback for my meditation practice) Few people experience the magic, because few actually push themselves to the necessary level.

### sfast — 2023-12-01T14:13:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328830>

It seems that there is a threshold for me: If I can stay 10min in 32°F without shivering or my feet start to hurt, something unlocks.

### sfast — 2023-12-02T08:17:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328845>

I do my CT first thing in the morning between 0515 and 0530. I look into the night's sky from my balcony. Cold seems to remove my mind from my body similar to meditation. Ad Astra is the best depiction of that feeling of void. (I love it!)

### sfast — 2023-12-03T18:27:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328881>

Strange observation:

1\. I started to supplement with Beta-Alanine because of both the empirical evidence and experience reports by Mat Fraser and alike. (Mat Fraser was the inciting incidence)
2\. The more I am cold adapted, the more the tingling side-effect of Beta-Alanine seems to kick in.
3\. When I am surprised (like when my daughter almost hits her head while falling), the tingling is out of this world.

### sfast — 2023-12-04T10:25:03+0200

The best time for me to do my prayers is actually my ice bath in the morning. The equation is pretty simple:

Ice bath = connection/unity with nature.
Praying = connection/unity with god.
Ice bath + Praying = Connection/unity with nature + god.
Nature = The material (materia shares the morpheme with mater (mother) perspective on existence.
God = The spiritual perspective on existence.
Praying in ice bath = Connecting the material and spiritual connection/unity to Unity.

### sfast — 2023-12-06T08:09:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328924>

Today 15 min in 32°F with no shivering. I feel that the onset of shivering is the best indicator of my cold adaptation.

### kris90 — 2023-12-06T20:54:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-328933>

Interesting thread man. Have you read Jack's CT-6 blog? [https://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-6-the-ancient-pathway](https://jackkruse.com/cold-thermogenesis-6-the-ancient-pathway)

I think it would connect a lot of dots for you, and I have noticed similar effects, although this time of year as we approach the winter solstice, I am much less active. The blog is really about how when we are cold adapted, we become ultra-efficient from a metabolic and performance standpoint. We can eat small, lift big and have insane bursts of power and strength should we need to use it.

I myself have stopped lifting weights a little over a year ago (used to be a bodybuilder). Right now, I walk 5 km a day, and maybe once or twice a week do some sprints. I'll do a 5 mins explosive calisthenics workout once or twice a week as well. CTing daily, 55 F tub for 5-10 mins seems to be all I need to keep the pathway open (I am extremely lean ~8% BF), and then embracing the cold weather throughout the day, as well as eating a carnivore diet with the odd bite of dark chocolate. I've also noticed since I've been pretty strict with my sleep patterns (usually going to bed around 7 PM), I seem to have naturally adopted a biphasic sleep pattern where I get my 2 phases of sleep: Phase 1 - (about 4-5 hours, very deep sleep), Phase 2 - (heavy dream state/in and out of consciousness).

I totally agree with your mention of CT being a form of meditation; I get that too. Cold environments are all about slowing down and reflecting and restoring and I think that's the magic of this pathway, restoring our systems to optimal.

I've also noticed since I've been pretty strict with my sleep patterns (usually going to bed around 7 PM), I seem to have naturally adopted a biphasic sleep pattern where I get my 2 phases of sleep: Phase 1 - (about 4-5 hours, very deep sleep), Phase 2 - (heavy dream state/in and out of consciousness).

This is very interesting. Currently, I experience a similar sleep pattern. During the first half of the night, I sleep very deep. Second half, I start to dream very intensively. First, I was worried about my overall energy. But I seem to do just fine.

### sfast — 2023-12-11T12:09:42+0200

I still have warm hands up to 10 minutes in 43°F water. (I have them outside the barrel because I am very tall and need to get into a strange position so my back is touched by the water). Also: After 15 min I don't need any warm up. My body reheats after a couple of minutes on its own.

### kris90 — 2023-12-13T16:32:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-329181>

Curious about your feeding patterns with the CT you're doing? I would assume your body is craving lots of fats? I find I'm always looking for ways to get more fat into me, probably because I barely have any fat of my own, but it's not a ravenous sort of hunger/craving, more of an absolute delicacy. I noticed my protein intake goes up too instinctively from doing CT, especially in my last meal of the day. Must be an mTOR thing.

### sfast — 2023-12-14T09:03:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-329212>

> [kris90 said:](/goto/post?id=329181)
>
> Curious about your feeding patterns with the CT you're doing? I would assume your body is craving lots of fats? I find I'm always looking for ways to get more fat into me, probably because I barely have any fat of my own, but it's not a ravenous sort of hunger/craving, more of an absolute delicacy. I noticed my protein intake goes up too instinctively from doing CT, especially in my last meal of the day. Must be an mTOR thing.

My day approximately looks like this:

0500 Waking up
0520 CT
0540 Running in Tank Top + Walking with my dog
0630 Strength (4x/week) or Mobility (2x/week)
**0700 2 TS MCT Oil**
0700 Desk (every 45min 2-3min Mini-Workout)
**1100 Snack (Cheese, Nuts, Vegetables, Fish)**
1200 Crossfit or Stability Circuit
**1230 Lunch (5-7 eggs, Bacon, Vegetables)**
1330 Walking my dog
1500 Desk
**1800 Supper (500-700g meat/cheese + Vegetables)**

I don't count macros any more. But from my experience (I counted _diligently_ for some time), I'd estimate my intake being 4500 kcal/day coming almost exclusively from protein/fat. Sometimes, I add a little bit of carbs, a slice of self-made oat bread (sometimes my daughter randomly stuffs a piece of banana in my mouth, costing me the last percentages of my optimised life. )

This year, I noticed a similar craving like you. Sometimes, in the evening, I feel that I need an additional snack. But when I eat some carbs, it feels very dissatisfying. I think it is just habitual from the summer. Right now, my hunger feels in a way distant. When I eat fat and protein, I feel a calm satisfaction, but sometimes I forget to snack and I am fine, too.

### sfast — 2023-12-16T09:34:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-329296>

\- Yesterday, I reached overreaching. So, it is time to back off. My method is simple: I half the volume of all my sessions.
\- During the last 3 weeks, I lost quite some bodyfat, but not bodyweight. I am not too lean yet.
\- But it might be that during the last second half of the winter, I need to artificially increase my fat intake (mostly likely by eating smetana).
\- It might be that I will introduce mini-refeeds during the week if the fat intake is not enough. I can perform independent on my carb intake. However, occasional winter carbs increase my regeneration and I seem to re-enter ketosis (measured by feeling) within half a day.
\- My heart rate went down to 46 in the cold, 48 while sitting and having drank a big cup of coffee. So, I am in a good trajectory for my resting heart rate. Right now, it is a bit elevated (51) because of overreaching.

I am wondering how to change CT during my deload week. I think I will be safe for now and reduce it to 5-10 min. Then I might try to keep it up while reducing the rest of my training to test for the stress CT puts on my body. I'd be happy if CT would be compatible with deloading. Then I'd up CT (20-30min in 32°F) since deload means that my training will be shorter, therefore giving me more room for CT.

### sfast — 2023-12-17T05:48:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-329315>

Another observation: When I sleep under an open window and the wind is going over the area between my shoulder blades, I paradoxically heat up and cannot sleep with a blanket covering more than my legs (one of them) and my arse. (my bedroom is ~57°F) But if the blanket is completely covering me, I can sleep just fine.

### sfast — 2023-12-17T15:48:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-329319>

There is one problem with my assumption that my heart rate will go down: I might put a bit too less stress on my heart.

It is:

\- 840 min in Zone 1
\- 232 min in Zone 2
\- 180 min in Zone 3
\- 120 min in Zone 4

### kris90 — 2023-12-20T20:43:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-329374>

I personally don't think winter is a time to focus on PRs and gains in performance. I think it is a time to slow down and repair and regenerate after a long light cycle. I think that's kind of what cold and CT does, and obviously there's more darkness, so more melatonin. I personally find doing CT alone keeps me pretty ripped/jacked without doing much (if any) physical exercise.

### coho3131 — 2023-12-21T02:08:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-329375>

Longevity & Health versus Performance
It is not uncommon for distance runners to more easily set PRs in the winter.
Distance running is probably not healthy for the average person unless the mental benefit would outweigh the physical stress.
Distance running might be healthy if you have the right levels of redox and heteroplasmy.

[https://www.amazon.com/Farm-Innovators-H-429-Economical-Submergible/dp/B000HHSI1E/ref=sr\_1\_26?crid=30VQAVEFJ6SUI&dib=eyJ2IjoiMSJ9.rkS6o2eZRgawU8Yn2Gq4l3OPvycPzNlDi6ngXlwiLxV7RsFzCjtN0e6ytZWYhpy4EKzLKiio5oRK1UMvwr4kvQ.Xd1IvWwVKZxTdeP8qR87yl2XcgtrZK-AzG1Nzl-5mbU&dib\_tag=se&keywords=stock+tank+de+icer&qid=1704985201&sprefix=stock+tank+de+icer%2Caps%2C106&sr=8-26](https://www.amazon.com/Farm-Innovators-H-429-Economical-Submergible/dp/B000HHSI1E/ref=sr_1_26?crid=30VQAVEFJ6SUI&dib=eyJ2IjoiMSJ9.rkS6o2eZRgawU8Yn2Gq4l3OPvycPzNlDi6ngXlwiLxV7RsFzCjtN0e6ytZWYhpy4EKzLKiio5oRK1UMvwr4kvQ.Xd1IvWwVKZxTdeP8qR87yl2XcgtrZK-AzG1Nzl-5mbU&dib_tag=se&keywords=stock+tank+de+icer&qid=1704985201&sprefix=stock+tank+de+icer%2Caps%2C106&sr=8-26)

Thanks you for the tip. I'll need to calculate the energy cost.

### Anthony Smith — 2024-01-12T20:36:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-330139>

> [sfast said:](/goto/post?id=330125)
>
> Thanks you for the tip. I'll need to calculate the energy cost.

A fish tank heater would do the same thing, keep the water from freezing. Be sure to unplug it (or take it out) before getting in the water, I expect the electric field from such a device will be significant. Do you have insulation around the barrel? Could help.

### kris90 — 2024-01-15T18:03:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-330263>

> [sfast said:](/goto/post?id=330125)
>
> Thanks you for the tip. I'll need to calculate the energy cost.

I believe they have auto temp-sensors so they shut off at a certain temp, but for my small tub, 2 hours is plenty to de-ice it.

### sfast — 2024-02-05T08:22:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-331026>

Currently, I am experimenting with adding 50 hand 2 hand swings with a kettlebell every 45 minutes for the first half of the day. I estimate doing 300–500 swings per day _additionally to my training program._

I shat on kettlebells as training tools for a decade because dumbbells are more versatile. Now, I am swinging away. I must correct myself for a specific reason: The kettlebell is attractive. It might be that my lizard brain sees colour, or I just like new shiny things. But my wife said that she is lucky that she can live with that one addiction: The addiction to swings.

In any case, one of the most important building blocks of my training philosophy is to never sit (or stand or lie) for more than an hour with no break. The training volume is almost _for free._ My subjective heuristic of the toxicity of sitting (additional to the known science) is that I get surprisingly cold-sensitive when I sit for more than 2–3 hours.

This is in my opinion one of the most important parts of designing a high volume training: Very tight fatigue control. Zone 2 cardio + strength training right at the MED for intensity + [intervals spread throughout the day](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Exercise-Snacks%3A-A-Novel-Strategy-to-Improve-Health-Islam-Gibala/e271093d5ffa14fa25337f0418ac86739577fe3b) = very little fatigue/stress. Right now, the only (psychologically) high intensity training are my two sessions in which I do circuit training to stimulate muscle hypertrophy (which is I never did before I approached 40). Even my sprinting, jumping and throwing is done in a relaxed and calm mental state.

### sfast — 2024-02-05T09:19:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-331027>

Today, I calculated my daily energy expenditure: 5223 kcal.

This is slightly below my rule of thumb that you should approximate a daily energy flux of 50kcal/kg, which is derived from the energy flux of hunter-gatherers.

### coho3131 — 2024-02-05T18:58:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-331051>

Have you read about repetitive motion or overuse syndromes?

For myself, I don't do a specific exercise more than every other day, and usually less frequently than that.

### sfast — 2024-02-09T07:35:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-331258>

> [coho3131 said:](/goto/post?id=331051)
>
> Have you read about repetitive motion or overuse syndromes?
>
> For myself, I don't do a specific exercise more than every other day, and usually less frequently than that.

Yes, I have.

ROS is very specific to a certain kind of problem that arises with thousands and thousands of identical movements, overloading intricate areas like the hands. It doesn't apply to a couple hundred easy reps.

### sfast — 2024-02-13T09:47:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-331390>

So far, I am very pleased with my increased training volume via swings and other kettlebell exercises throughout the day. A sign of healthy spinal erectors is their width: If you grab them with your hand on your back, they should feel _wide_. Over the last couple of years, I felt my erectors didn't lose depth but width. This is because I engaged with less and less rotational stressors (martial arts, floreio, etc.).

Right now, my swing _variant_ library is limited to a dozen variants that I cycle through. But as a fan of the basic idea of [Wolfgang Schöllhorn,](https://sport.uni-mainz.de/files/2014/05/tws_DifferenziellesLernen.pdf) I try to create intra-set variability: I am consciously repositioning my feet after each rep or try to achieve a certain feel etc.

But the width of my erectors is coming back nicely, which I feel in the general resilience of my back: If somebody asks me to help to move, I only mind the time lost, but never the social connection or the physical stress. Because of the way I trained my back, moving (carrying odd objects over a long period of time) becomes a very nice and pleasant stimulus for my back. The next day, my back feels better than before.

### sfast — 2024-04-17T10:17:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sprinting-jumping-throwing-as-a-cold-adapted-mammal-trying-to-make-sense-of-my-phenotype.28297/post-333684>

So far, I am very pleased with the results of this winter. I had to shorten this winter's cold training because of logistics. However, these are some observations:

\- I am approaching all-time PR realm in strength. I don't peak anymore (no true 1RM-Tests, no reps over 90%1RM or something like that). However, in the next months I will most likely hit some training PRs in upper body lifts
\- I have normal high speed endurance. I can still stair-jump five steps at once non-stop for 20–30 minutes (I am not rushing, just slowly walking down the stairs after the jump)
\- My body fat is coming down. (I never had high body fat, so the change is from low to very low)
\- This year I have no issues with pollen allergy (in the past, a by-product of being cold-adapted in the winter)
\- Work Capacity is high as expected from the addition of Zone 2 cardio and cold training.
\- The joy of warm weather is REAL. Early ice bathing, running in sleet with a tank top, rising so early that it is dark for hours after waking. I don't mind this that much. I even had the impression of becoming night-adapted. But now, I start my day in the dark, but the sun rises soon. I get into a mild state of euphoria if the sky is even clear.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: antenna search map
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antenna-search-map.28298/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### coho3131 — 2023-11-06T15:20:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antenna-search-map.28298/post-327910>

Do the towers generally transmit more nnEMF than the antennas?

### Anthony Smith — 2023-11-06T18:03:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antenna-search-map.28298/post-327922>

Just different frequencies. I understand towers to be mainly phone 3G, 4G & (maybe) 5G. You will see a phone company name next to many. Antennas are mainly radio: police, fire, taxi, transport companies, radio stations etc. You will see the name of the company, local council etc next to many of these. Both distance and power need to be considered. Small towers put out less that big towers.

The Sutro Tower in San Francisco is huge and has EVERYTHING on it, I’ve read the the incidence of cancer within 2 miles of the Sutro Tower is 5X the normal and 2X as far as 5 miles out. Years ago (3G days) I read the incidence of cancer in a small town in Europe with one “small” cell tower was 2x within 400 meters (1,200 feet) but normal beyond that.

### coho3131 — 2023-11-06T22:17:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antenna-search-map.28298/post-327952>

> [Anthony Smith said:](/goto/post?id=327922)
>
> Just different frequencies. I understand towers to be mainly phone 3G, 4G & (maybe) 5G. You will see a phone company name next to many. Antennas are mainly radio: police, fire, taxi, transport companies, radio stations etc. You will see the name of the company, local council etc next to many of these. Both distance and power need to be considered. Small towers put out less that big towers.
>
> The Sutro Tower in San Francisco is huge and has EVERYTHING on it, I’ve read the the incidence of cancer within 2 miles of the Sutro Tower is 5X the normal and 2X as far as 5 miles out. Years ago (3G days) I read the incidence of cancer in a small town in Europe with one “small” cell tower was 2x within 400 meters (1,200 feet) but normal beyond that.

Thank you!

# Thread: Thiamine for someone following a semi-SAD
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thiamine-for-someone-following-a-semi-sad.28299/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TaylorF — 2023-11-06T16:01:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thiamine-for-someone-following-a-semi-sad.28299/post-327912>

Hello,

I'd like to start my mother on a regiment of magnesium and thiamine. She is pre-diabetic (doctor recommended trying Wegovy), only about 25-30 lbs overweight, she has issues with her nervous system, neck, and vagus nerve trouble (vasovagal symptoms).

I'm working on getting her to turn off the wifi and leave her phone off in the kitchen at night, and have her wearing blue blockers after sunset. She eats a fairly standard diet, protein bars or egg bites for breakfast, turkey, cheese, snacks with lunch, and usually shrimp and vegetables or a salad for dinner, she also likes sugar, usually in the form of hot cocoa or an organic cookie brand.

I'm wondering what dose of thiamine and magnesium I should have her start taking, especially since she takes vitamin d with k2 and I've gotten her levels up to 60, but that's mostly through supplements.

Thanks for any and all tips.

Taylor

### JanSz — 2023-11-06T16:40:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thiamine-for-someone-following-a-semi-sad.28299/post-327915>

pre-diabetic= high fasting insulin
Work on her to eventually arrive at eating only within 4-6 hours and during daylight.
Think also about modifying intake of major protein sources from meat to vegetables,
eating less meats much more legumes.
One major reason farmed (organic or not) animals, birds and fish contains insane amounts of linoleic acid.

Get her a scale that show weight and body fat.
Tell her to go on that scale and check it, every time she wants to eat.
\--------
vitamin d with k2
Make sure that k2 contains Menaquinone-7 or MK7
also add Serrapeptase, or nattokinase or lumbrokinase.
Serrapeptase is least expensive

Vit D (and A) are better when eating
Belveder Premium Wild Cod Liver in Own Oil
buy a lot of it at one time, on amazon, they will cost same as in store or less but will be much fresher.

///////////////
If you live in high population density, and not able to relocate:
do not worry about minute details of nnEMF, spend your energy on other things.
...

### Anthony Smith — 2023-11-06T18:36:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thiamine-for-someone-following-a-semi-sad.28299/post-327925>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=327915)
>
> ///////////////
> If you live in high population density, and not able to relocate:
> do not worry about minute details of nnEMF, spend your energy on other things.
> ...

I could not disagree more . . . living in a high population area even more reason to mitigate the nnEMF you can control, inside the home. TaylorF, do what you are doing AND turn off the circuits in her bedroom at night to reduce/remove the 60Hz AC field. Get her a battery clock & small (red) flash light to see (if she has to get up). Reducing the nnEMF will make everything else you are doing 2x more effective.

>

Does she eat pork? A great source of thiamine. When I use thiamine (benfotiamine) with clients, I start them at 250mg with food.

### JanSz — 2023-11-06T20:42:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thiamine-for-someone-following-a-semi-sad.28299/post-327941>

I thought that all people who live in high (made of steel) towers are doomed, maybe not.

### Anthony Smith — 2023-11-06T21:04:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thiamine-for-someone-following-a-semi-sad.28299/post-327947>

> [JanSz said:](/goto/post?id=327941)
>
> I thought that all people who live in high (made of steel) towers are doomed, maybe not.

The steel in the building goes deep into the ground so the whole building is grounded plus built to commercial code so all the wires are metal clad. I was taking readings in my local HomeDepot, went to the lighting dept. - NO electrical field. All the light are attached to the metal shelves which are bolted to the ground. There are 2 cell towers a hundred yards away, on the garden side of the building - little to NO RF signal in Home Depot . . . The metal mesh cage of the garden area blocked the signal (think screen door on a microwave oven). Don’t guess, you don’t know unless you measure.

Make sure the case only shields on one side and have the shield between them and the phone when using. Only works when speaking on the phone not looking at the screen doing email/text etc. With the back open the phone runs on much lower power.

### JanSz — 2023-11-06T23:09:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thiamine-for-someone-following-a-semi-sad.28299/post-327965>

> [TaylorF said:](/goto/post?id=327953)
>
> How does one test for linoleic acid levels in the body?

Very important question.
Do not use any indicators or evaluators.
Test them directly and draw your own conclusions.
For myself I use this test:

Fatty Acid Profile, Comprehensive (C8-C26), Serum

Then read results and calculate this relation:
LA/ALA

LA-Linoleic Acid (Omega-6)
ALA-Alpha Linolenic Acid (Omega-3)
//////////

# Thread: Are There Health Benefits of Being VERY Cold Adapted?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-there-health-benefits-of-being-very-cold-adapted.28301/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sfast — 2023-11-07T11:22:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-there-health-benefits-of-being-very-cold-adapted.28301/post-327971>

Hello from Germany!

I am slowly transitioning from performance-oriented training to a more health-oriented approach. Meaning: I try to optimise my performance for the long-term and not just short-term.

To me, there are seemingly no downsides to CT compared to physical training. Sure, I need to watch out during the individual session to not get hypothermia. But I don't push to a point of me shivering anyhow.

So, if the short-term risks a mitigated and long-term adaptation us secured, there is only one question left: Is there a cut-off threshold at which I don't improve my health but just train to be able to tolerate cold for longer?

Live long and prosper
Sascha

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2023-11-07T17:10:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-there-health-benefits-of-being-very-cold-adapted.28301/post-327980>

I thought shivering during CT was a good sign...??

### Jack Kruse — 2023-11-07T18:39:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-there-health-benefits-of-being-very-cold-adapted.28301/post-327989>

If you arent tightly coupled yep.

### Saichi — 2023-11-07T19:35:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-there-health-benefits-of-being-very-cold-adapted.28301/post-327994>

How long are your CT sessions currently and at what temp?

### Anthony Smith — 2023-11-07T20:11:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-there-health-benefits-of-being-very-cold-adapted.28301/post-327998>

Not my area so this is more of a question - from what I have read here I am led to believe - about 50º for 15 mins is all it takes?

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2023-11-07T20:24:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-there-health-benefits-of-being-very-cold-adapted.28301/post-328003>

> [Anthony Smith said:](/goto/post?id=327998)
>
> about 50º for 15 mins is all it takes?

I think optimal time and temp are entirely dependent on your current health status and your goals for health

I started my water CT this week. So, just 8 min @4°C (~40°F). Because of time constraints, I will keep my water based CT sessions below 30 mins. Then I'll add cold air directly afterwards for 30 mins. The temperature is whatever is outside early in the morning.
