# Thread: Wall During a Marathon
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wall-during-a-marathon.28302/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JanSz — 2023-11-07T17:03:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wall-during-a-marathon.28302/post-327979>

### How to Calculate and Predict When You’ll Hit the Wall During a Marathon​

### [How to Calculate and Predict When You’ll Hit the Wall During a Marathon - Runners Connect](https://runnersconnect.net/marathon-hitting-the-wall/)

"Hitting the marathon wall" is a much-feared and much-discussed phenomenon in marathoning. Runners speak ominously about a sudden wave of fatigue that sets in at about 20 miles into a marathon. At this point, they say, the race is half over. Contending with the wall is a problem whether you're...

 runnersconnect.net

\-----------

#### John Davis​

“_Hitting the marathon wall_” is a much-feared and much-discussed phenomenon in marathoning. Runners speak ominously about a sudden wave of fatigue that sets in at about 20 miles into a marathon. At this point, they say, the race is half over.
Contending with the wall is a problem whether you’re an elite runner or recreational jogger.
Thanks to research by exercise physiologists, we know that runners “hit the wall” because they run out of carbohydrates stored in their body and have to suddenly shift to burning mostly fat to keep them going ([recap on the science of bonking and glycogen depletion](https://runnersconnect.net/running-training-articles/cience-of-bonking-and-glycogen-depletion/))
Understanding where the wall comes from is one thing, but predicting it and determining how to overcome it is a bit more challenging.
Today, we’ll take a look at the efforts of one researcher to develop a mathematical model which can predict whether you’ll hit the wall in your next marathon, and where exactly you’ll hit it.

## Rapoport’s Energy Consumption Model​

The researcher, Benjamin Rapoport, is an MD-PhD candidate at Harvard and MIT. In 2010, he published a paper which described his model for tracking the energy consumption of a marathon runner.
The premise of Rapoport’s model is fairly simple: it takes a certain amount of energy per mile and per pound of body weight to move a runner from point A to point B. Converting to metric, this amount is conveniently about one calorie (as in food calories) per kilogram of body mass per kilometer of running.
**This would mean a 150-pound runner requires around 110 calories per mile, regardless of pace.**
But, there are also two more variables to consider above simply weight and distance:

### Energy expenditure from fat​

In Rapoport’s model, the per-mile energy expenditure of runners doesn’t vary based on the speed they run, but _where_ they get their energy from does.
Though runners mostly think about carbohydrates as their principle energy source, a significant portion of your energy expenditure while running a marathon comes from fat.
At easy paces, you burn more fat; as you run faster, your body begins to rely more heavily on carbohydrates.
So, on a typical easy run,
**you might be burning 60% carbs and 40% fat.**
But in a 5k race,
**well over 90% of your energy is going to be coming from carbs**
—you just can’t burn fat quickly enough to produce enough energy to [run 5k pace](https://runnersconnect.net/5k-and-10k-training/).
Keep in mind that _**hitting the wall**_ **happens when you run out of** _**carbs**_ (your fat supply is effectively unlimited), so to predict if and when this will happen, we need to know **how fast** you’re running.

### Carbohydrates​

The final variable in the mathematical model estimates how much energy you have stored up in your body in the form of carbohydrates.
There are two sources for your body to access carbs when you run: **carbs stored in your liver, and carbs stored in your leg muscles.**
According to Rapoport, the size and energy density of the liver are tightly regulated by the body, so this amount is only dependent on your body mass (as the liver makes up about 2.5% of your weight). The energy stored in your leg muscles also depends on your body mass, but also the size of your legs and the density of carbohydrates stored in them.

## The Equation​

Rapoport provides a range of typical values for each of these: for most people, leg muscles make up about 21% of their body mass, and a typical runner stores about 36 calories worth of carbs per pound of muscle.
By doing some simple algebra, we can create an equation to calculate how far you can run before you expend all of your carbohydrate energy and hence hit the wall.
Once you’ve used up your energy stores in your liver and your legs, you can go no further—at least, not without a massive shift in carbohydrate consumption and likely a massive drop in speed as well.

## Calculating When You Will Hit The Wall​

Rapoport’s model is best displayed as a graph, reproduced below.
The only inputs you need to figure out are the relative speed you intend to run your marathon at, expressed as a percentage of your VO2 max, and the amount of carbs stored up in your leg muscles. Surprisingly, body weight doesn’t factor into the final result: although heavier runners require more energy to cover a given distance, they also (at least in this model!) have more carbohydrates stored up in their body.
Figuring out your pace as a percent of your VO2 max sounds tricky, but it’s not too hard.
We can use your pace in an all-out two-mile race as a pretty good estimate of your VO2 max pace, then figure out your marathon pace as a percent. Haven’t run the two-mile since high school? Multiply your 5k time, in minutes, by 0.63 to get an estimate of what you can run for two miles.
Let’s say you’ve run 25:00 for the 5k and are hoping to run your next marathon under 4 hours in 3:55

1.  An 25:00 5k multiplied by 0.63 gives us about 15:57 for two miles, or 7:39 mile pace—that’s your VO2 max speed.
2.  Dividing your VO2 max pace by your goal marathon pace (9:00 per mile) tells us the relative intensity you’ll be running at. In this case, it’s 0.83, or 83% of your VO2 max. According to the scientific literature, most people are able to run a marathon at 60-85% of their VO2 max.

Elites and experienced runners tend to be on the higher end of this scale, and less experienced runners tend to be on the lower end.
Figuring out how much carbohydrates your muscles have stored up is more complicated—it’s not something we can estimate with easy-to-measure numbers like your 5k PR. Instead, we’ll create three different scenarios:

-   A conservative estimate which assumes our leg muscles don’t have very much stored carbs,
-   A middle-of-the-road estimate which uses typical carb storage values from scientific studies of endurance athletes, and
-   A generous estimate, which uses carb storage values from “carbo-loaded” athletes who have consumed a lot of carbs before their event.

With this information, we can predict a range of points when you’ll hit the wall.
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

In the graph above, the red curve depicts the conservative estimate, the orange curve depicts the typical estimate, and the green curve depicts the generous or “carbo-loaded” estimate. The gray shaded region represents the typical range of speeds that marathoners can maintain for the duration of the race—60-85% of their VO2 max.
Once we know what percentage of VO2 max pace we wish to run at for our marathon, we can draw a vertical line to each curve to determine where we’ll hit the wall in each scenario.
In our example from earlier of the 25:00 5k runner shooting for a 3:55 marathon, we know that he’s going to be running at 83% of VO2 max. In this case, drawing a vertical line (marked on the graph in dark gray) up from 83% of VO2 max pace shows that, in the conservative scenario, our runner will hit the wall at a bit over 17 miles into the race.
If his muscle energy stores are more in line with that of a trained athlete, he could make it all the way to 23 miles before he hits the wall. And if he’s done some carbo-loading in the days before the race and has above-average carbohydrate storage in his leg muscles, he could make it all the way to the finish without hitting the wall.

## Conclusion And Limitations​

Rapoport concludes his study with a few words of caution: this model _is_ a simplistic one, and glosses over some important details.
To name just one, this model assumes that all runners have about the same amount of muscle mass in terms of percentage of their body weight—something that isn’t true in the real world!
**Rapoport calculates that the uncertainty associated with his model should be around 5-10%, meaning our predicted “wall” timings could be off by one or two miles.**
Considering this, it’s best to use the conservative estimate—the red line—as a starting point for planning out your next marathon race. Also keep in mind that even this conservative estimate might be off by a mile or two.
So, you’ve done the math, checked the chart, and found out that you’re probably going to hit the wall in your next marathon. But what can you _do_ about it—how do you figure out how much carbs to take and when to take them? That’s what we’ll look at next time!

#### Who We Are​

Your team of expert coaches and fellow runners dedicated to helping you train smarter, stay healthy and run faster.
We love running and want to spread our expertise and passion to inspire, motivate, and help you achieve your running goals.

GET STARTED NOW

### References​

1\. Rapoport, B. I., Metabolic Factors Limiting Performance in Marathon Runners. PLoS Computational Biology 2010, 6 (10), e1000960.
2\. Maughan, R. J.; Leiper, J. B., Aerobic capacity and fractional utilisation of aerobic capacity in elite and non-elite male and female marathon runners. European Journal of Applied Physiology and Occupational Physiology 1983, 52 (1), 80-87.
3\. Daniels, J., Daniels' Running Formula. 2 ed.; Human Kinetics: 2005.

### JanSz — 2023-11-07T17:15:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wall-during-a-marathon.28302/post-327981>

Assume healthy 150# 5'8" toll, male running marathon and achieving the best to date marathon time.
How much energy he spends running that marathon?
Post that in kWh and HPh.
\------------------------------------------
A marathon is approximately 42.195 kilometers (26.219 miles).
The energy expenditure for running depends on the weight of the person and the distance they run.
[For a **person weighing 150 pounds** (approximately 68 kilograms), the total energy required to run a marathon is approximately **2950 kcal**](https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1000960)**.**
[This is based on the energetic cost of running, which is approximately 1 kcal per kilogram of body mass per kilometer of running](https://runnersconnect.net/marathon-hitting-the-wall/).
Now, let’s convert this energy expenditure to kilowatt-hours (kWh) and horsepower-hours (HPh).
1 kcal = 0.00116222 kWh So, 2950 kcal = 2950 \* 0.00116222 = **3.428 kWh**
1 kcal = 0.00155961 HPh So, 2950 kcal = 2950 \* 0.00155961 = **4.601 HPh**
So, a 150-pound male running a marathon expends approximately **3.428 kWh or 4.601 HPh of energy.**
Please note that these are approximate values and actual energy expenditure can vary based on numerous factors including the runner’s efficiency, the course profile, and environmental conditions.

fastest marathon time

[who completed a marathon in](https://www.statista.com/statistics/1298762/fastest-men-marathon-times/)
[**2 hours, 0 minutes, and 35 seconds** in Chicago in 2023](https://www.statista.com/statistics/1298762/fastest-men-marathon-times/)

### JanSz — 2023-11-07T17:24:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wall-during-a-marathon.28302/post-327982>

### [Should You Become a Fat Adapted Runner - Runners Connect](https://runnersconnect.net/should-you-become-a-fat-adapted-runner/)

Training isn’t the only part of Ultra or Marathon Racing you need to concern yourself with. In fact, the nutritional component can be as, if not more important, if you don't get it right. That said, there's an emerging trend among ultra and marathon runners to switch their diets to primarily...

 runnersconnect.net

### Should You Become a Fat Adapted Runner​

#### Coach Jeff​

Training isn’t the only part of Ultra or Marathon Racing you need to concern yourself with.
In fact, the nutritional component can be as, if not more important, if you don’t get it right.
That said, there’s an emerging trend among ultra and marathon runners to switch their diets to primarily fats, rather than carbohydrates, to become more “fat adapted”.
So, in this article we will discuss what that means, if it’s effective, how to do it, and whether you should try to become a fat adapted runner yourself.

### What Does It Mean To Be Fat Adapted​

Let’s start by examining some basic nutrition principles.
In order to run, your body needs to burn energy. Luckily, our body has **two primary forms of energy – carbohydrates and fats.** Unfortunately, both also have pluses and minuses.
Carbohydrates are burned very efficiently by the body since you can quickly break them down into usable energy.
The downside is that you can only store a finite amount of carbohydrates (not even close to enough to get you through a race) and therefore you need to be constantly replenishing them.
Fat, on the other hand, isn’t broken down by the body very efficiently. As such, the more energy your body demands (i.e. the faster you want to run) the harder it becomes for your body to use far.
The plus-side, however, is that you have more than enough fat stored in your body to last the entire race!
**Therefore, when we talk about becoming a fat adapted runner,
this means training the body to burn fat more efficiently as a fuel source.**

### Levels Of Fat Adaptation​

Now, there are also different “levels” of fat adaptation you can strive for accomplish.
On one end of the spectrum,
you have those that are living in a constant state of ketosis. This is when you constantly restrict carbohydrate intake so that you’re producing ketones (molecules your body uses to burn energy)
This is the highest level and when your body will become the most efficient at burning fat when you run.
On the lower end of the spectrum,
you have runners that only practice fat adaptation during their runs by fasting for 12-18 hours before their workout and not fueling during.
This will result in better fat burning adaptation, but not as much as a runner in ketosis.
In the middle of the spectrum
would be runners who adhere to a low carbohydrate diet at all times and who practice **fasting during their runs.**
The important distinction here is between a “low carbohydrate diet”, which usually means around 150g of carbohydrate intake, and ketosis, which is less than 50g of carbohydrates per day.

### Will Being Fat Adapted Help You Burn More Fat?​

Unfortunately, right now there is not a lot of research on ketogenic diets and endurance performance.
The ultimate study would be able to compare two runners performing the same distance at the same effort and measure the percentage of fats vs carbohydrates burned and cross section this with performance metrics like heart rate and running economy.
But, here is what we do have available right now.
One of the best studies on whether fat adapted diets do increase fat utilization was conduced by Stephen Phinney and Jeff Volek in 2016 (Fat-Adapted Substrate use in Trained Elite Runners).
This study showed that keto-adapted athletes burned around **1.2g/min of fat**, which is a far greater percentage than **the .76g/min** we normally see marathoners burn.
Furthermore, a study in 2016 by Tim Noakes compared the fat-burning ability of a keto group vs a “normal” higher carb group in a 2-hour ride at 70% VO2max.
Researchers found the keto group did indeed burn more fat (1.2g/min) compared to the high carb group (0.5g/min).

### You Can Burn More Fat, But Does That Lead To Better Performance?​

Okay, so now we know that fat-adapted athletes do indeed burn a greater percentage of fat when training and racing.
**But does this come at a cost to performance? Are the athletes working harder or need to slow down to achieve these results?**
That’s what the research group at the Australian Institute of Sport, led by Dr. Louise Burke PhD, set out to test
The study involved creating **three different groups of race walkers:**
a high carb group,
a carb restricted group and a keto group
and having them compete in both a 10km and 25km time trial.
The results once again demonstrated that the keto had higher rates of fat oxidation compared to the higher groups.
But, here’s the important part.
The keto group performed worse than there first 10km test
while the high-carb and periodized-carb group improved their race time by 6.6 and 5.3% respectively.
And in the 25km event,
the keto group had higher rates of exertion and higher heart rates
than the carbohydrate groups.
**To put more clearly, in 10km and 25km events, athletes who adhered to a ketogenic diet performed worse** than those who used a low carb or normal carb diet.
The researchers posit this happened because running economy is impacted when in ketosis.
While the body burns fat more efficiently, it still has to work hard to do so.
Now, a critical thing to keep in mind here is that the longest event was 25km.
Moreover, the participants were racewalking, not running.
So, we don’t fully understand how this data will translate to running events between a marathon to 100 miles.

### The Pros And Cons And Should You Try To Become Fat Adapted?​

My experience working with thousands of runners is that you should choose a diet option that best suits your lifestyle and personality.
A fully ketogenic diet is difficult to maintain and comes with its own set of challenges.
For some runners, the discipline required to maintain the diet is a welcome challenge that keeps the motivated. Likewise, some runners may feel healthier overall when they are on a ketogenic diet.
So, if you enjoy the ketogenic diet and feel strong and healthy, there’s no harm.
But, if sticking to such a rigid diet is difficult or you don’t feel like you have enough energy, then you certainly don’t need a ketogenic diet to benefit your racing.
As the research indicates,
from a pure performance perspective,
a full ketogenic diet will not enhance your performance.
In fact, Dr. Burke and her team recommend a more balanced approach to fat adaption, stating …

> A periodized programme that includes some training sessions deliberately undertaken with low carbohydrate availability or a delay in replacing muscle glycogen after a session may promote greater cellular adaptations to training and enhance performance to a greater magnitude than undertaking all sessions with high carb availability.

Based on my own coaching experience and the data gathered by research, I agree with this approach.

### How And When To Perform Fat Adapted Training​

We cover this more in our [Race Day Fueling Course](https://runnersconnect.net/marathon-nutrition-blueprint/), but here is a simple set of rules to follow…
If you are first starting with fat-adapted training, you should run your early training segment long runs in a glycogen depleted state.
I don’t recommend trying it for the first time on your first 20 or 22-miler.
This is because (1) we want to ease your body into the process to see how it reacts and (2) if you do struggle, we don’t want it to be during an important long run or workout.
Thus, I recommend using a fat-adapted strategy as early in the training cycle as possible and then up until your last two or three race specific long runs.
During these last few race specific long runs, I recommend practicing your race fueling strategy instead.
This will ensure you have ample time to tweak anything you need for race day.

### What Does This Mean For Your Race Day Fueling Plan​

The important part of all this information is really how you out it in practice.
If you are a member of our [Training Plans](https://runnersconnect.net/pro-signup/), [Masters](https://runnersconnect.net/pro-signup/) or [Marathon Nutrition Blueprint](https://runnersconnect.net/marathon-nutrition-blueprint/), here is how you adjust the specific calculators to account for the level of fat-adaptation training you employ.
For the marathon, you don’t change your fueling numbers at all.
This is because at the intensity of the marathon, here’s only a slight percentage point difference in how much carbohydrate you’re going to need to replace.
For the ultra marathon, we provide you two sets of data. The carbohydrates needed for a more traditional diet and the carbohydrates needed for a fat-adapted athlete.

-   If you practice no fat adaption methods, then choose the higher of the carbohydrate numbers.
-   If you practice some fat adaption methods, use the lower carbohydrate number.
-   If you consistently practice fat adaption methods in training and have a lower carbohydrate diet, you can use the high end fat adapted output.
-   If you are always in a state of ketosis, you can use the lower end of the fat-adapted calculation.

I hope this article helped you understand some of the science behind fat adaption training and how to best implement it in your training.

# Thread: How is weight loss achieved
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-is-weight-loss-achieved.28304/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 3

### ateale — 2023-11-08T17:10:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-is-weight-loss-achieved.28304/post-328027>

Hi everyone!

I have a friend who seems interested in some of the ideas related to restoring one's circadian rhythm.

He asked me how it's possible for people to be losing so much weight by simply improving their light diet and getting exposure to cold (CT).

This is what I currently understand more or less but I would appreciate any further notes/ideas/corrections so that I can pass it on to my friend and hopefully convince him to join these forums:

**Sunlight**
\- Exposure to blue light outside of sunlight hours ruins/interrupts circadian rhythm
\- Blue light stimulates blood glucose and insulin
\- Blue light turns off POMC (pro-opiomelanocordon) and expression whereas UV light increases POMC (Sunlight stimulates POMC which leads to the creation of melanin)
\- Red light preconditions your skin for UV

**Cold Thermogenesis**:
\- body generates heat when exposed to temperatures significantly below its optimum temperature
\- it accelerates various metabolic processes, such as energy creation, fat burning, digestion, and muscle activity
\- Water condenses, causing the condensation to contract around mitochondria make it more energy efficient and increases electrical and magnetic fields, causing us to burn more calories

**Eat like a great white shark**
\- proteins / fats contain more more (and less deuterium than compared to carbs/sugars) allow for our mitochondria to produce more water that is lower in deuterium ?

**POMC**
\- gene or group of hormones???
\- regulates melanin produced from melanocytes in the skin
\- POMC levels are regulated by sunlight (effected by time of year / season / location)

### ateale — 2023-11-08T17:17:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-is-weight-loss-achieved.28304/post-328028>

Cold exposure stimulates leptin too?

**Leptin**
\- underneath our subcutaneous fat
\- is responsible for regulating the energy balance in the body - communicates with the hypothalamus -> suprachiasmatic nucleus (central clock of the body?) + pineal gland?

**Melatonin**
\- produced by pineal gland when melanopsin isn't signalling to it (this happens at night / when there is not sunlight)
\- tells your body that it is nighttime, cell repair / cleanup begins

### JanSz — 2023-11-08T17:55:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-is-weight-loss-achieved.28304/post-328033>

I have a friend who seems interested in some of the ideas related to **restoring one's circadian rhythm**.
\=================================================================
One thing about good circadian rhythm is good cortisol levels over the night and day.
One of promoted ways of achieving that is having early morning outdoor exposure.
(On purpose I am not mentioning that we must look at the actual sun.)
\-------
Testing often is discouraged, but I like to test.
I like DUTCHtest.com
\----------------------------------
When you do that test,
you may find out that your cortisol pattern is weak.

**But keep looking at your test results.**
You may find out that your adrenals are **making heaps of cortisol**
but
most of it is immediately converted to cortisone (the inactive part).

Chewing on licorice may adjust that conversion toward your benefit.

### Emily Burgoon D.O. — 2023-11-09T01:17:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-requesting-recommendations.28306/post-328057>

Hi,
One of my greatest frustrations in this journey so far is that I haven't been able to get either of the martial arts gyms I train with to do anything about the terrible light over their training mats. Does anyone have a favorite podcast or educational resource on the indoor exercise topic that might be convincing? Or experience with similar and how to get their attention? I figure if I could get the message across that they would have a competitive advantage in their fights it would elicit a change, but so far they've ignored my communications about this. Thanks...

### coho3131 — 2023-11-09T02:08:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-requesting-recommendations.28306/post-328058>

Unfortunately, I think that is going to be very challenging to accomplish.
Exercising indoors under artificial light affects dopamine and other pathways in unhealthy ways, and it is very addictive.
A lot of us have followed the Erwan LeCorre pathway and now only do our dedicated physical activity outside, even when the weather is not comfortable.
You and your friends could try to start your own outdoor martial arts meeting place.
I wish you good fortune.

# Thread: Hello from Appalachia (with a wee touch of "woo")
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-appalachia-with-a-wee-touch-of-woo.28307/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kalikaye — 2023-11-09T03:04:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-appalachia-with-a-wee-touch-of-woo.28307/post-328061>

Hello! I have been lingering around for a couple of months, consuming and digesting information - podcasts, videos, blogs, forums. It has been a maginificent unfolding.

My name is Samantha. I live in the heart of Appalachia on the western side of the mountains, nestled in the foothills. I turned 31 two weeks ago. Once upon a time, I was a yoga teacher and a painting/art instructor in the Washington, DC area. Then I moved to the mountains for love of a great man, and became a farmer/gardener. Now, for our meat and potatoes, my partner and I work in insurance and travel domestically at random and unknown intervals for the contract-type job. When I'm not doing that, I still farm and garden on a small scale, and sometimes my partner and I mix in some international travel. When we have "free time", we also build installations and paint murals together, sometimes for festivals/events/parties, and sometimes just for fun. I'm still a devoted student/practitioner of yoga and classical tantra, which, oddly enough fits so perfectly into the quantum mosaic Jack is unveiling. I am in awe at every "aha" moment (which happens several times a day these last couple months). It's a different language, but they say the same thing, and I can feel this beautiful joining happening deep within me.

I want to share a story that might be a little "woo" for many here, but it is how I came to be here...

In August, my partner and I went to western New York to help set up a very small grassroots music festival with some acquaintances we had met last year. During the tear down, we went out to dinner one night with some of these new friends. On the drive, one of them looked at me randomly and asked if I had heard about "light language." I thought about it for a moment, and remembered an experience I had at an ecstatic dance 7-8 years ago, where a woman asked to share a song during the closing. It was one of the most beautiful things I had ever heard, and the experience of listening to her sing was profound. But the profundity must have worn away during the years, I suppose, because I had not revisited it until prompted. I replied - yes, I think I have. And he just kind of smiled and looked out the window. It was random and strange. The conversation ended and I did not think of it again.

About two weeks later, my partner and I were working in TN. I was at our airbnb doing computer work. I got up to make lunch and do some laundry, and started humming while I worked. The humming grew into a song of foreign sounds and syllables, grew into a full blown song of a complete and perfect different language. It was coming out of me without thought, and really, beyond any control or force of my own. I'm not sure how else to explain it. I told you it was woo, remember?

This song really took over me, took over my whole body, and I moved through the house singing and dancing and moving with my eyes closed and surrendered to...whatever this was...during this time, I had a feeling of being fully present in my body, in the space, in the house, but at a completely different level. It felt cosmic and minute at once. And I saw something, like a vision. This vision showed me all about LIGHT. What Light really was, how it really moved, how it really worked, that light could be DENSE, could have MASS and FORM, that it had structure and what that structure was. How it created literally all things. And how it was so abundant in this reality, but we could not fully access it without better understanding it. This is the simplest, shortest explanation I can give of what I was shown. With the visuals, came a lot of information, but it was not in words. It was just a knowing. I didn't (still don't) have any words to articulate what information came into me during this time. I just _knew_ more information about reality, light, space, LIFE, breath, nature, movement, CREATION; I could just _feel_ it.

I later (thanks to the internet) found out the experience I had was called a "light language activation." Believe it, or not, woo, or not. I reached out to the friend who had asked me randomly about this thing, and thanked him. He sent a cascade of quanta into motion that led to this opening in me (and ultimately led me here). I have been working with this ever since, and it continues to show me and teach me things at this level of innate feeling/knowing. But no words. No vocabulary or real context.

And then, about two weeks after this experience, I found a podcast interview with Jack (can I call you Jack?) from several years ago, and within about 10 seconds of hearing him speak, I felt that same thing. I felt the TRUTH of it. He was speaking the WORDS of what I had learned in the light langauge activation. (How's that for entanglement?). So I dove in. I've been diving in, listening and reading as much and as often as I can between the obligations of daily life.

Since finding Jack, an insanely complex and beautiful quilt has been coming together. Many previously disparate threads from early childhood through to now are"making sense" and I am seeing their links. I can feel the clink of things falling into their place and finally coming together into some larger orchestration, every piece building something greater...each particle making the one before it and after it make sense. I'm developing a clear understanding of my life and LIFE in general. It's tingly. It's powerful. I'm excited for this unfolding. I'm a little daunted too, but I know that's just one small egoic part. I can FEEL the truth of this knowledge in every cell, and now I'm working on getting my rational/verbal mind to catch up.

It feels like my brain is learning the vocabulary for a language my heart/body has been speaking forever. I know no other way to explain it at this point. I have no science or medical background whatsoever. I can't define or produce the technical vocabulary that I hear or read, but I understand it implicitly when I do. I can even SEE in my head all the pieces, parts, organs, etc. as I ingest, and the rational part of my brain going "oh - that's _this"_ labeling everything as we go.

So for now, I am learning to speak this language, and more importantly perhaps, I am _feeling_ this language. My brain is getting on the board, and soon, the totality of me will be riding the wave...

I am trying to be slow with the information so I can really absorb and understand it, but also move quickly, as I feel an increasing sense of urgency and importance of this knowledge for the world, for humanity, for me.

And at times in the last couple of months, I have asked myself - damn, what took me so long to get here?

But today, I am just grateful that I am.

Thank you for receiving my long-winded hello. I am looking forward to learning with all of you here.

p.s. I love BOOKS and am always looking for great recommendations (non-fiction or fiction). I've started acquiring and reading some of the books recommended here - The Body Electric, Life on the Edge, The Fourth Phase of Water, and What is Life?, just to start - but I would love to hear about any other books that have really ignited you or brought things together in that "click-yes-truth" kinda feeling (any genre).

### Sue-UK — 2023-11-09T09:32:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-appalachia-with-a-wee-touch-of-woo.28307/post-328067>

Hi Samantha and welcome to the forum!

I've had experiences of taking consciousness to different places in the cosmos and meeting beings of light, and at one time earned my living from being able to gain information intuitively. The more woo the better for me.

Today's lesson on biophysics of consciousness: The better your mitochondrial thermodynamics the better cognitive abilities are. The more consciousness one has.

Light illuminates the reality of what time and space are to the human experience. Time and space are mere illusions of our perceptual abilities. If you use phsyical forces in Nature we can induce consciousness and we can make it disappear right before your eyes even as nothing on Earth really changes to prove this point. How? Watch the video. Time & space are constructs used by humans to better understand and operate in our world. That there is no such thing as an "external" and "internal" world and what we think we see "out there" is literally occurring and being conceived inside our head at the ATPase level.
Based on physics and biology, this science is unfortunately reality; it turns out that our ability to handle to use sunlight to handle the atomic mass in our ATPase that allows us to be consciousness. That sole ability that dictates all that we see and perceive in our world.
Proof that this counterintuitive idea is true?
This is time lapsed photography of what happens in neurodegeneration in older humans that over decades. It does not have to take that long if the atomic mass is added at a faster rate in a younger brain. This young brain experienced the increased mass via momentum of the force of gravity induced by velocity and not the weight associated with the atomic mass of deuterium.
Deuteriums higher mass effectively mimics the increase G forces in your ATPase and your brain fails under that weight or mass. Consciousness declines to neurodegeneration and cognitive decline before your brain fails in death.

# Thread: Timing of UVA rise; UVA transition
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-of-uva-rise-uva-transition.28309/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3

### enyaw — 2023-11-09T16:35:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-of-uva-rise-uva-transition.28309/post-328077>

I imagine that the time of the UV-A transition every morning varies bases on season, latitude, and altitude. Would a simple way to determine this timing be my weather app? If the UV index is 0 does that mean no UV is present and when it goes to UV 1 there is UV-A present? Does anyone have any better suggestions? Is there a site that displays this information? Would a cheap UV monitor from Amazon be accurate enough to determine the proper time? I like cheap options that don't require internet access.

### Ted — 2023-11-10T00:21:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-of-uva-rise-uva-transition.28309/post-328118>

The easiest is Dminder app. When the UV index goes from 0 to 1, that's when the UV A came in

### Anthony Smith — 2023-11-10T00:53:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/timing-of-uva-rise-uva-transition.28309/post-328120>

Someone else might could verify this but I read as a rule of thumb, if your shadow is longer than you are tall (sun below 45º) there is no UV.

# Thread: WAS AND IS CO2 levels critical to primate evolution?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/was-and-is-co2-levels-critical-to-primate-evolution.28310/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2023-11-09T18:57:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/was-and-is-co2-levels-critical-to-primate-evolution.28310/post-328086>

If modern humans were wise, they would not be wearing sunglasses blocking their RPE. When you do, you ATPase spin rate slows. Since you are your ATPase doesn't spin well = cognitive issues = bad thinking = allows for bad ideas. The decentralized science =

[https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1722639050723438811](https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1722639050723438811)

\= your decentralized free lesson has ended.

Do not believe in anything tied to climate change.

### Jack Kruse — 2024-05-11T23:43:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/was-and-is-co2-levels-critical-to-primate-evolution.28310/post-334635>

THE REAL REASON FOR CLIMATE CHANGE: The warming from the Sun is cyclical, it's NOT constant. The distance of where you are from the Sun is constantly changing because both the Earth's orbit around the Sun is irregular and the Sun itself wobbles due to the combined gravitational pull of all the planets together. Look at the Schwabe solar cycle of 11 years, the Jose solar cycle of solar Inertial Motion of 179 years, Eddy Solar Cycle of 1000 years, the Bray-Halstatt Cycles of 2300-2500 years, then look into the three Milankovitch Cycles. Short to medium term climate change: Solar Inertial Motion: the combined mass of the planets also moves the position of the Sun through their combined gravitational pull, meaning the Sun moves around following the ever moving barycentre of the Solar system rather than being in a fixed central point in the middle Solar System. That is the key thing to understand: the Sun is moving around, wobbling in spiral like motion as it travels, it is not stationary. Once you understand that all medium term climate change can be explained simply because of the Sun's changing distance from the Earth. None of this has anything to do with humans. None of this has anything to do with CO2. The models of the Solar System you grew up believing as a child were gross over simplifications. They conditioned you to believe that the Solar system has a fixed Sun position with a regular Sun activity with regular orbits, of which the Earth is one. Yet that is not the reality: not only the earth both tilts and wobbles as it orbits, but the orbit is a changing ellipse not a perfect circle, meaning the distance from the Sun is not constant. These are the three Milankovitch cycles. Also other planets have irregular orbits. The combined effect of all these irregular orbits together pulls the Sun off centre of the solar system into the barycentre. A wobbling Sun is the real reason for short to medium term climate change, and an irregular earth orbit, tilt and wobble is the reason for long term climate change. And this is just the beginning of the story of irregularity in the Earth's orbit around the Sun, then there are cycles of Sun activity, making it stronger and weaker according to how close to the 11 year cycle of magnetic poles flip it is, next being in 2024, and how many Sun spots & Solar flares we are exposed to. Then you need to factor volcanic activity, the Hunga-Tonga Hunga underwater volcanic eruption of January 2022 increased the water vapour in the stratosphere by 10%, this in itself will cause considerable warming of the planet in most regions. It's definitely not a simplistic neat black and white story of CO2, a minor greenhouse gas, as 95% of the earth's greenhouse gases are constituted by water vapour instead. Humans have no power to determine either the orbit of the Earth around the Sun or the Sun's internal & external activity, or the water vapour in the atmosphere. Life adapts much more easily to higher temperatures and increases in CO2, particularly plants, vegetation, trees, plankton& phytoplankton, than it does to decreases in CO2. The real danger is a decrease of CO2, and a decrease in temperature, not an increase in either. Once again, we have been deceived by a systematically corrupt scientific funding system linked to oligarchs interests. CO2 was always a control knob for economic prosperity, not climate.

### Jack Kruse — 2024-06-09T19:45:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/was-and-is-co2-levels-critical-to-primate-evolution.28310/post-335616>

This is nature’s CO2 scrubber. It’s called a tree it’s renewable and it works via photosynthesis powered by sunlight. There is no climate crisis. There is a crisis in science education and understanding that the global elites are using to book profits from the ignorant. Many corporate interests are in on the elite shell game. We need to out them and consume them for compost of our CO2 scrubbers.

# Thread: Treating Spine Patients in Northern Latitudes with Decentralized Framework AKA (WINTER)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/treating-spine-patients-in-northern-latitudes-with-decentralized-framework-aka-winter.28312/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### dr.g — 2023-11-09T20:21:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/treating-spine-patients-in-northern-latitudes-with-decentralized-framework-aka-winter.28312/post-328104>

Decoupling from the Sun occurs in the winter months in the norther latitudes. I grew up in Wasilla, Alaska. The winters are long and dark. The land dies and is then reborn. Alaskan natives (depending on the tribe) lived on salmon, whale blubber, seal meat, berries, and fermented food. They had almost undetectable levels of artherosclerotic disease when first studied by westerns. Most have tan skin and were cold exposed constantly. In winter, the reflection from the snow can cause sunburns, so enough UV gets through if you're outside long enough.

Now practicing in Seattle, where modernity meets biological reality. High levels of depression, osteoporosis, obesity, gender dysphoria, autism. I didn't realize the spectrum of disease until I came back from the south. I trained in Arizona and at LSU in New Orleans. The pathology here is different.

The problem identified:
How do we address the evolutionary mismatch of low light in the winter to humans who are not adapted to this level of solar radiation due to differences in melanin concentration, and who are also not cold adapted, and are all blue light toxic due to high tech industry workers, and or from constant phone stimulation.

Example: A 36 year old patient comes to my office with worsening low back pain. MRI L-spine shows modic changes at L4/5 and L5/S1 and disc degeneration.
Centralized treatment:
Physical therapy for lumbar stabilization, core strengthening.
NSAIDs
Muscle relaxers
Epidural steroid injections

There is no other treatments that are covered by insurance generally speaking. The condition is chronic. The spine will continue to degenerate. The primary complaint is physical pain. As a physician who generally is treating pain, my goal is a reduction in pain and suffering and increase in functional activity and overall well being.

What can be offered from decentralized thinking? If it is winter time and a working class person goes to work when it is dark and comes home when it is dark. What can be done to maintain energy balance and offset lack of sun?

Open to any and all input

What blood work can be done to track improvements or changes?
hs-CRP
Vit D
SED rate
TSH Free T4, T3

Some thoughts:
Infrared Red Sauna vs Normal Hot Sauna
Light therapy (photobiomodulation) - recommendations for commercial beds or devices?
Methylene blue
Only eating local seasonal food
Cold thermogenesis protocols

Business Concept: Low light areas need Membership Based Healing Centers focused on
\- restoration of circadian cycles via photobiomodulation ( brain and omental signaling)/whole body
\- light chambers (red light, infrared)
\- Sauna, steam room
\- exposure to green areas
\- hot yoga
\- meditation
\- decentralized nutrition counseling

Alternatively, a subscription based wellness home kit that includes photobiomodulation devices, access to video library content from decentralized physicians by specialty, membership access to network with other wellness focused individuals.

Anyone know of such a concept being implemented or want to help build healthcare 3.0 infrastructure?
Looking for mastermind brain trust to create vision, protocols, mission statement and innovate.

The north needs better healthcare.

In response to the original post as well I'm curious how thorough of an assessment this patient had beyond the imaging? # Thread: Diabetic retinopathy stoichiometry defines most traumatic brain injuries.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diabetic-retinopathy-stoichiometry-defines-most-traumatic-brain-injuries.28313/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JanSz — 2023-11-09T20:55:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/diabetic-retinopathy-stoichiometry-defines-most-traumatic-brain-injuries.28313/post-328107>

Person with diabetes has a high fasting insulin.
Question:
**What fasting insulin level healthy person should have?**
I am hearing that **it should be 2-3** but definitely less than 5.5.
What is your opinion? Or what are you aiming for yourself?

LabCorp
2.6−24.9 μIU/mL

### [004333: Insulin | Labcorp](https://www.labcorp.com/tests/004333/insulin)

Labcorp test details for Insulin

 www.labcorp.com

\---------------------------------------------------------------------
The quote:
Diabetic retinopathy stoichiometry defines most traumatic brain injuries.
is from your post here:

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tbi-thread.26146/#post-328090](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tbi-thread.26146/#post-328090)

\----------------------------------------------------------------------------
**Diabetic retinopathy** is a complication of diabetes that affects the eyes. It is caused by damage to the blood vessels of the retina, which is the light-sensitive tissue at the back of the eye. [This damage can lead to vision loss or blindness if left untreated](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fopht.2022.1012804/full).

**Traumatic brain injury (TBI)** is a type of brain injury that occurs when an external force, such as a blow to the head, causes damage to the brain. [Symptoms of TBI can range from mild to severe and can include headaches, dizziness, confusion, memory loss, and more](https://www.aao.org/eyenet/article/traumatic-brain-injury-visual-disorders-what-every-2).

One of the major reasons for high blood pressure is being older than 40yo.

[According to different sources, **the percentage of adults over 40 who have high blood pressure ranges from 33.2% to 74.5%**](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=16b3272e275461c4JmltdHM9MTcwMTEyOTYwMCZpZ3VpZD0zMTBiZjgxOS0wYTdmLTZhMTEtMGU1Mi1lYmNhMGJkNzZiNjgmaW5zaWQ9NTc3MA&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=3&fclid=310bf819-0a7f-6a11-0e52-ebca0bd76b68&psq=half+of+the+people+with+high+blood+pressure+are+usually+40+years+old+and+older&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYWFycC5vcmcvaGVhbHRoL2NvbmRpdGlvbnMtdHJlYXRtZW50cy9pbmZvLTIwMTcvY2RjLWJsb29kLXByZXNzdXJlLWZkLmh0bWw&ntb=1)[1](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=8ef58d613d6abc9cJmltdHM9MTcwMTEyOTYwMCZpZ3VpZD0zMTBiZjgxOS0wYTdmLTZhMTEtMGU1Mi1lYmNhMGJkNzZiNjgmaW5zaWQ9NTc3MQ&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=3&fclid=310bf819-0a7f-6a11-0e52-ebca0bd76b68&psq=half+of+the+people+with+high+blood+pressure+are+usually+40+years+old+and+older&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYWFycC5vcmcvaGVhbHRoL2NvbmRpdGlvbnMtdHJlYXRtZW50cy9pbmZvLTIwMTcvY2RjLWJsb29kLXByZXNzdXJlLWZkLmh0bWw&ntb=1)[2](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=64333728bfcff447JmltdHM9MTcwMTEyOTYwMCZpZ3VpZD0zMTBiZjgxOS0wYTdmLTZhMTEtMGU1Mi1lYmNhMGJkNzZiNjgmaW5zaWQ9NTc3Mg&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=3&fclid=310bf819-0a7f-6a11-0e52-ebca0bd76b68&psq=half+of+the+people+with+high+blood+pressure+are+usually+40+years+old+and+older&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuY2RjLmdvdi9uY2hzL3Byb2R1Y3RzL2RhdGFicmllZnMvZGIzNjQuaHRt&ntb=1).

\-----------
The immediate reason for high blood pressure are:
lack of nitric oxide in the blood or just lower NO levels, lover than optimal.
Resulting in rigid (not-expanding, not-flexible) cardiovascular system.
Think how the car would behave if you removed shock absorbers. Or welded them solid.

The other are deposits on the interior surfaces of cardiovascular system, (reducing available volume.

I suspect that ADH (Antidiuretic Hormone), vasopressin
levels play's role too (by adjusting total blood volume level).

Also, body volume must be playing significant role.

Body bones amount and cardiovascular system volume
I think are rather steady.
But in 1900 average man was weighting 140 -150#
in 2023 that is usually above 200#

Statins increase cardiovascular calcifications.
(and reduce cardiovascular volume, increasing need for higher blood pressure).
\-------------
Thank you to abundance of (bad) food.
Thank you to seed oils.

# Thread: A little overview over different training modalities
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-little-overview-over-different-training-modalities.28318/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sfast — 2023-11-11T14:31:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-little-overview-over-different-training-modalities.28318/post-328152>

Hi Folks,

this image (copyright by me) is part of the generalised model of fitness I am working on:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### Some commentary:​

\- This is why most bodybuilders are sick and why their intuition to do fasted cardio first thing in the morning is right: You need to earn Zone 4 by being fit. Otherwise you can't support higher intensity stuff.
\- The energy usage is a statement of an ideal situation. If you are sick, you will burn carbs even in zone 1. This provides a rough guideline how to progress the training. You start by increasing Walking, NEAT (Crafting is AWESOME exercise), get your leptin sensitivity etc. Then you introduce Jogging, Dancing, Light Exercise like Yoga etc. Wait until you can burn fat there, too. Then Strength + Athletic Training (it can be scaled to almost any situations) but keep the training mode towards low reps (intensity as needed), high sets. And then you might earn your way towards Zone 4.
\- CT w/o shivering is Zone 1/2, CT + Shivering is Zone 3. So, this might contradict Dr K. Either my model needs to be worked, I contradict Dr K or I am wrong and shivering is still Zone 2.
\- Notice that strength is CP + Fat. No high reps with fatigue to be found. In my experience, this increases exercise tolerance for people who want to train a lot or cannot train a lot because of sickness.
\- Zone 1/2 and big part of Zone 3 is outdoor exercise by nature.

### John Warner — 2023-11-11T18:20:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/volcanic-redox-power-on-display.28319/post-328179>

Reading about the poles shifting. 2050 is the window. Aurora are seen as far south as Mexico and Arizona? earth quake, volcanic activity< crazy weather. Won't be none of this just survival, or thriving for some.

### JanSz — 2023-11-11T18:40:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/volcanic-redox-power-on-display.28319/post-328181>

The Earth’s poles are indeed shifting, and this is largely attributed to climate change.
[The geographical poles—the north and south tips of the axis that the Earth spins around— wobble over time due to small variations in the sun’s and moon’s pulls, and potentially to motion in Earth’s core and mantle](https://www.pbs.org/newshour/science/nasa-earths-poles-are-tipping-thanks-to-climate-change).

[Around 2000, the pole took an eastward turn; it stopped drifting toward Hudson Bay, Canada, and started drifting along the Greenwich meridian in the direction of London](https://www.pbs.org/newshour/science/nasa-earths-poles-are-tipping-thanks-to-climate-change)[1](https://www.pbs.org/newshour/science/nasa-earths-poles-are-tipping-thanks-to-climate-change). [The team found that recent accelerated ice loss and associated sea level rise accounted for more than 90 percent of the latest polar shift](https://www.pbs.org/newshour/science/nasa-earths-poles-are-tipping-thanks-to-climate-change).

However, there’s no specific prediction or scientific consensus that the poles will shift significantly by 2050.
[The process of pole shift is gradual and has been happening over centuries](https://www.businesstoday.in/visualstories/news/earths-pole-is-shifting-heres-how-it-can-affect-us-and-our-planet-55030-10-08-2023). [I](https://futurism.com/1-evergreen-what-happens-if-the-earths-magnetic-poles-flip)
[t’s also important to note that even if the Earth’s magnetic poles flip, scientists believe that the magnetic field would not completely disappear](https://futurism.com/1-evergreen-what-happens-if-the-earths-magnetic-poles-flip).

[Please note that while **pole shifts have happened many times in Earth’s history**, predicting exactly when the next one will occur is currently beyond our scientific capabilities](https://www.edgarcayce.org/about-us/blog/blog-posts/cayce-s-pole-shift-prediction-speeds-up/). It’s a complex process influenced by a multitude of factors, many of which are still being studied.
\=======================================
\=======================================
[The last significant geomagnetic reversal, known as the Brunhes–Matuyama reversal, occurred approximately 780,000 years ago](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geomagnetic_reversal). **This event is when the Earth’s magnetic field last underwent a full flip.**

[As for the existence of humans,](https://www.khanacademy.org/humanities/world-history/world-history-beginnings/origin-humans-early-societies/a/where-did-humans-come-from)
[**Homo sapiens, the species to which modern humans belong, evolved between 200,000 and 300,000 years ago**](https://www.khanacademy.org/humanities/world-history/world-history-beginnings/origin-humans-early-societies/a/where-did-humans-come-from)**.**
So, humans were not present during the last pole shift.
They appeared much later in the Earth’s timeline.
**[The development of civilization as we know it is only about 6,000 years old](https://www.universetoday.com/38125/how-long-have-humans-been-on-earth/)****.**
**Therefore, no human civilization has experienced a full geomagnetic reversal.**

////////////////////////////////////////

### Anthony Smith — 2023-11-11T19:24:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/volcanic-redox-power-on-display.28319/post-328182>

Thanks for highlighting this Jack, this is something we need to keep an eye on and be prepared for (extra food etc) because one or two big volcanic eruptions could trigger a global winter, mini ice age event. CT for everyone, like it or not . . .

This has happened multiple times in recorded history. The year 1816 is known as the Year Without a Summer because of severe climate abnormalities that caused average global temperatures to decrease by 0.4–0.7 °C. Summer temperatures in Europe were the coldest of any on record between the years of 1766 and 2000. This resulted in major food shortages across the Northern Hemisphere. Evidence suggests that the anomaly was predominantly a volcanic winter event caused by the massive 1815 eruption of Mount Tambora in April in the Dutch East Indies. This eruption was the largest in at least 1,300 years; its effect on the climate may have been exacerbated by the 1814 eruption of Mayon in the Philippines.

I’m not concerned about a pole shift, everything said about them is pure speculation.

I also used this plastic to build window inserts for a window in every room of my house.

### Phil — 2023-11-29T19:25:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phil%E2%80%99s-journal.28326/post-328779>

370nm UV A spotlight. Got a waveform 365 spotlight, the peak light output is 368 so pretty much 370. There is no flicker on these lights. You can hardly even tell this light is on during the day because most of the light isn’t visible. I also have tried out the 395 waveform spotlight but it kicks out a lot more visible light and is kind of annoying on the eyes. I know the intensity is pretty strong with these based off how bright the 395 spotlight was even during the day. So if you get one of these be careful. I’ll use these by shining up or at a wall to get the light reflecting off the wall or I’ll place this a few feet above me where I won’t get the light directly in the eye.

We replaced the window to the left with UV open greenhouse plastic so we are likely getting most the UV and IR light passing through it.

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2023-12-17T18:28:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phil%E2%80%99s-journal.28326/post-329325>

> [Phil said:](/goto/post?id=328779)
>
> We replaced the window to the left with UV open greenhouse plastic so we are likely getting most the UV and IR light passing through it

I was thinking some more about this UV-open plastic earlier this week. My understanding is that many visibly transparent and translucent plastics out there can be very opaque to NIR wavelengths.

Have you tested this particular plastic to see if it is transparent to NIR wavelengths specifically?

If it isn't, this plastic may be doing more harm than good...

If you are getting all the UV, but none of the NIR...

### coho3131 — 2023-12-18T17:14:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phil%E2%80%99s-journal.28326/post-329335>

Agree. Filtered sunlight = artificial light (nnEMF)

### Anthony Smith — 2023-12-18T18:58:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phil%E2%80%99s-journal.28326/post-329342>

We all agree that outside, full spectrum sun light is best . . . But when inside (on a hot or cold day) and open windows are not an option, is filtered window light (maybe balanced with some artificial UV/NIR better than no light?

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2023-12-18T21:51:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phil%E2%80%99s-journal.28326/post-329349>

> [Anthony Smith said:](/goto/post?id=329342)
>
> But when inside (on a hot or cold day) and open windows are not an option, is filtered window light (maybe balanced with some artificial UV/NIR better than no light?

I think any light during the day is better than no light. Sitting in a cave all day is a quick way to derail your circadian rhythm.

A primary concern with indoor environments though, is that even with windows open and lights turned on, it can still be akin to sitting in a cave. Indoor light is simply no match to the intensity of light outdoors. Our bodies NEED intense light during the day (except when near winter solstice)

But yes, I would say some is superior to none at all.

Now to optimize the light further:
It is important that the light your are getting has IR to balance the oxidative effects from higher energy photons.

The tricky part is ensuring that you are actually getting enough IR. The sun's rays are approximately 42% IR. I haven't tried this or tested it out personally, but I feel like that level of IR would be very difficult to replicate in an indoor environment.

I would absolutely augment with as much IR as possible if you have the lamps to do it though.

On the opposite end of the spectrum, we of course, need UV as well.

UVB is not important this time of year (outside of the tropics), but definitely need UVA year round.

So any UVA lamps will help as well.

I would be careful using any UV lamps in the absence of sufficient IR though.

### Anthony Smith — 2023-12-19T00:10:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phil%E2%80%99s-journal.28326/post-329351>

When I can’t be outside I am surrounded by windows and I have the equivalent of 1,000 watts of NIR light about 2 - 3 feet from me, (behind my iPad screen) + a UVA light + a full spectrum grow light.

### petrichor — 2023-12-23T02:09:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phil%E2%80%99s-journal.28326/post-329433>

Why not use an air curtain instead of all those lights? Open the window all the way, regardless of weather.

### Anthony Smith — 2023-12-23T04:11:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phil%E2%80%99s-journal.28326/post-329435>

> [petercowan said:](/goto/post?id=329433)
>
> Why not use an air curtain instead of all those lights? Open the window all the way, regardless of weather.

The lights are grounded, no electric or magnetic fields - Air curtain, let me see . . . $700 - $2100 (each) . . . a constant 60Hz cycle noise . . . Electric field extends 6 - 8 feet into the room . . . Magnetic field from the motor . . . And I would need several as the sun moves throughout the day . . . that’s why.

### petrichor — 2023-12-23T21:49:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phil%E2%80%99s-journal.28326/post-329442>

> [Anthony Smith said:](/goto/post?id=329435)
>
> The lights are grounded, no electric or magnetic fields - Air curtain, let me see . . . $700 - $2100 (each) . . . a constant 60Hz cycle noise . . . Electric field extends 6 - 8 feet into the room . . . Magnetic field from the motor . . . And I would need several as the sun moves throughout the day . . . that’s why.

Thank you. That was informative and those are very good reasons.

Putting cost aside for a moment, and please bear with me, I am quite new to this stuff. Could we:

Ground the air curtain as well.
Eliminate the 60hz cycle noise with an isolated power supply.
Use magnetic sheidling to redirect the magnetic field and effictively eliminate it in the indoor environment.

### Anthony Smith — 2023-12-23T22:24:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phil%E2%80%99s-journal.28326/post-329443>

> [petercowan said:](/goto/post?id=329442)
>
> Thank you. That was informative and those are very good reasons.
>
> Putting cost aside for a moment, and please bear with me, I am quite new to this stuff. Could we:
>
> Ground the air curtain as well.
> Eliminate the 60hz cycle noise with an isolated power supply.
> Use magnetic sheidling to redirect the magnetic field and effictively eliminate it in the indoor environment.

All good questions.
Yes the unit could possibly be grounded - but only by measuring can one know if or how well that worked. It depends on the internal wiring of the unit.
The noise from the unit I’m talking about is the sound it makes when running and you can’t cover the unit to suppress the sound because it has to be open top and bottom to “breathe”. The frequency/vibration of the fan/motor would be very draining. Think of the relief you feel when you turn off any motorized device. Any “DE” (noise) could be filtered, I expect they all run on 120V AC.
Magnetic shielding is very hard to do and the materials expensive IF it is even possible. The type of motor would determine how far the magnetic field extended.
In very hot, cold, wet weather the best solution would be to use non UV/NIR blocking/reducing plastic/glass.
Do you have meters to measure?

### Phil — 2023-12-26T15:24:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phil%E2%80%99s-journal.28326/post-329474>

> [Joseph LeBlanc said:](/goto/post?id=329325)
>
> I was thinking some more about this UV-open plastic earlier this week. My understanding is that many visibly transparent and translucent plastics out there can be very opaque to NIR wavelengths.
>
> Have you tested this particular plastic to see if it is transparent to NIR wavelengths specifically?
>
> If it isn't, this plastic may be doing more harm than good...
>
> If you are getting all the UV, but none of the NIR...

The plastic I used is UV open polyethylene, polyethylene is has a massive window for letting Infrared light through. polyesters, polycarbonates, and acrylics are some plastics thag are opeque to NIR. I prefer the plastic to even an open window because it diffuses the light. This means the sun is coming down at an angle at my window and the diffused light reflects that light all over my room instead of just a bright intense spot on my floor. This means the plastic is lighting up my house with brighter light than If I just had the window open.

I even have troubles believing the idea that most windows block NIR and UV A light. Based off what I’ve researched only newer windows with specific films block near infrared and UV A light. Standard glass blocks around 10-15% UV A and maybe the smallest amount of NIR light. My house is older so I have older windows, there is a chance me replacing my windows with plastic inserts has no added benefit. NIR light is the deepest penetrating frequency of light and easily passes through things if the other visible spectrum are passing through. Mid and far infrared light are the frequencies that generate a lot of heat on our skin because they don’t penetrate deeply and are absorbed at the surface of our skin, these frequencies are more likely to be blocked by a window and this is why a window gets warm/hot. Mid and far infrared light penetrate the greenhouse plastic far better than glass this is why I can sit in my greenhouse when it’s -10 degrees F and get hot and sweaty.

On top of having all my windows replaced with this greenhouse plastic I have the 370nm spot light blanketing my kitchen and living room along with a NIR spotlight and a BFP 9000 panel mounted to my ceiling blanketing my house it NIR light. [https://gembared.com/collections/full-body-lights/products/gembared-bfp-9000-body-light-led-panel](https://gembared.com/collections/full-body-lights/products/gembared-bfp-9000-body-light-led-panel)

### Phil — 2023-12-26T15:44:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phil%E2%80%99s-journal.28326/post-329475>

Using clear marbles as lenses on this low intensity panel is pretty awesome. The marbles really direct and focus the light. The marbles press into the skin pressing the skin pushes the blood that would normally block/absorb some of the light to get the deepest penetration possible. You have to be careful if you do this with another panel the marbles or glass orb intensifies the light more than 4x

### petrichor — 2023-12-30T04:42:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/phil%E2%80%99s-journal.28326/post-329616>

Thanks for explaining, Anthony.

I don't actually own an air curtain. I read about them in John Ott's Light and Health and thought it might be a novel way to get more sun inside on a cold day. Based on your response, it sounds like more trouble than it would be worth.

This means the sun is coming down at an angle at my window and the diffused light reflects that light all over my room instead of just a bright intense spot on my floor. This means the plastic is lighting up my house with brighter light than If I just had the window open.

> I even have troubles believing the idea that most windows block NIR and UV A light. Based off what I’ve researched only newer windows with specific films block near infrared and UV A light. Standard glass blocks around 10-15% UV A and maybe the smallest amount of NIR light. My house is older so I have older windows, there is a chance me replacing my windows with plastic inserts has no added benefit. NIR light is the deepest penetrating frequency of light and easily passes through things if the other visible spectrum are passing through. Speaking of turmeric onions garlic etc
and thinking of inflammation:

For the last about 150 years, farmers are feeding whatever living creatures they are feeding
them with remnants of seeds from which seed oil was taken out.
That goes for the conventional food and also for organic. That goes for venison that supposed to be meat from wild animals, but those animals are also fed by farmers.
Many know to not eat seed oils, most do not know that farmed animals, birds, fish, pigs etc. are fed with seed.
So, meats from them have very high content of LA, Linoleic Acid.
That causes inflammation in our bodies.

Not sure if knowing above or by coincidence, supercentenarians eat much less meat.
Which may among any other considerations
lessen the extremely high load of Linoleic Acid (Omega-6)

After that you should take good care of your stomach acid.
From my personal experience, very low potassium level was causing low gastric acid and the following pain and stress.
Many decades of suffering.
Then it turned up that one teaspoon of potassium bicarbonate was all that I was missing.
Cost likely about one USA cent over couple of days.
They also likely will keep telling you that your problems are due to overly high acid in your stomach.

Current medical system makes sure that you will not think about possibility of the lack of potassium.
They do that by testing (the wrong potassium practically every time you doctor orders blood test for you).

(Potassium RBC)

### Joseph LeBlanc — 2023-11-16T17:22:54+0200

### Dr. Marcus Ettinger — 2023-11-25T16:03:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori.28331/post-328643>

> [SahiraMarconi said:](/goto/post?id=328270)
>
> Hi ! I have active H. pylori but I’m not sure if go straight into antibiotics. Should I try any other treatment ? Does anybody have any information about that? Thank u so much

Sahiri,

Just because you have H. pylori does not mean it needs to be treated. 1/2 of the world's population has it right now. If you have an ulcer that was validated by endoscopy or a mother or father who had stomach cancer, then it should be treated. Your symptoms may be coming from something else and if that is the case, then when you get rid of the H. pylori the symptoms will still be there and on top of that, you will have subjected yourself to a round of 2 antibiotics. I have treated more than 300 people with H. pylori since 2007 and I know it backward and forwards. Im including two posts I wrote to give you more information.

### [The Most Effective Natural H. pylori Treatment Protocol](https://www.advancedhealing.com/heartburn-gastritis-achlorhydria-h-pylori/)

Since 2007 this is the most effective natural H. pylori protocol. H. pylori can cause gastritis, ulcer, SIBO, anemia, anxiety, and rosacea...

 www.advancedhealing.com

### [Dr. Ettinger's Biofilm Protocol for Lyme and Gut Pathogens](https://www.advancedhealing.com/biofilm-protocol-for-lyme-and-gut-pathogens/)

Biofilm Protocol and information for H. pylori, Lyme disease, prostatitis, sinusitis, Chlamydia pneumoniae, and gram-negative bacteria

 www.advancedhealing.com

### Jack Kruse — 2023-11-25T16:29:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori.28331/post-328644>

When you get it your redox is down in the organ of zuckerlandl.

### Saichi — 2023-11-18T00:18:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/travel-drive-versus-fly.28337/post-328393>

Nature provides everything we need. Nnemf is robbing you of what you think nature isn't providing you.

### coho3131 — 2023-11-18T01:27:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/travel-drive-versus-fly.28337/post-328395>

> [Saichi said:](/goto/post?id=328393)
>
> Nature provides everything we need. Nnemf is robbing you of what you think nature isn't providing you.

I agree, but practically nobody lives in Nature anymore.

Almost all of us live in an artificial environment.

Almost all of us use a phone, a computer, electricity, and other technology.

If a person lived far from the Equator without any technology, maybe for winter their stores of Vitamin D would be sufficient, but they would not be optimal.

Also, sun on the skin is very important for other reasons besides Vitamin D. (One example, apolipoprotein B and endothelial integrity)

Traveling near the Equator in winter is closer to optimal in a world filled with technology.

Eating seafood if you live far from the ocean is not "natural" either, but the iodine and DHA it gives is helpful because it can mitigate some of the effects of technology.

Maybe this is what you meant also. What do you think?

### Saichi — 2023-11-18T02:08:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/travel-drive-versus-fly.28337/post-328396>

You're just now getting emf meters. If you mitigate to Building Biology standards for your living spaces then high latitude isn't a problem. Less light in winter's balanced by more CT and what you perceive as suboptimal is nothing compared to nnemf damage.

I haven't eaten any Pacific seafood for 11 years now. I don't own a Geiger counter yet but it's already known the Pacific's filled with radionuclides. I do eat as much wild Atlantic seafood as possible though.

Traveling by car, train, or plane are nnemf bombs. It's getting very difficult to participate in society and not get sick.

### Jack Kruse — 2023-11-18T14:52:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/travel-drive-versus-fly.28337/post-328411>

Not eating Pacific seafood is irrational behavior.

### Anthony Smith — 2023-11-18T23:02:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/travel-drive-versus-fly.28337/post-328422>

The life style and practices of a healthy person can be (are) very different to a sick and/or sensitive person. I’m not going to stop eating peanuts because some people are allergic. Prevention (so as not get sick / allergic) is wisdom but each person has to find their own balance “happy place”. I believe the benefits of my morning swim in the pacific out weighs any potential negatives, AND if I glow in the dark I will need less nnEMF blue light . . . always think possative

### Saichi — 2023-11-18T23:38:15+0200

### Jack Kruse — 2025-04-02T17:01:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/travel-drive-versus-fly.28337/post-349967>

NO is why JanSz got banned. And then this...........

Today I give you a gift. It is my mitochondriac anniversary on April 2nd. I post this for my Mom. She used to call me at this time every year. Now i am calling you, my tribe. It is time to wake up.

[https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-40-124743154](https://www.patreon.com/posts/decentralized-40-124743154)

"The sage doesn't seek to be profound but is useful. A sage himself is a former fool who got tired of themselves."

This insightful quote paints a picture of wisdom born not from an innate desire for grandeur but from a humble journey of self-awareness and growth. A decentralized sage, in this light, is not someone who sets out to dazzle the world with lofty thoughts but rather an individual who, through the weariness of their own folly, discovers a practical clarity. Their usefulness emerges from their centralized experience, mistakes made, lessons learned, and a quiet resolve to move beyond the foolishness of their centralized past. It’s a reminder that true depth often comes not from chasing profundity but from the simple, steady act of outgrowing one’s former self.
